itineraries.title: Historic Cities of Minas Gerais - Colonial Gold Circle
itineraries.subtitle: Eight days in the heart of Brazil's mining state, following Aleijadinho's trail and the 18th-century gold rush from Ouro Preto to Diamantina.
itineraries.notes: Depart from Belo Horizonte (Confins airport), return to Belo Horizonte. Roads are in good condition but winding and mountainous—allow 30% extra time versus Google Maps. Ouro Preto, Mariana, and Tiradentes are entirely cobbled; proper shoes essential. Tropical highland climate (800-1200m): mild days but cool nights year-round, bring a sweater even in summer. Diamantina is 4h30 north, prioritize 2 nights there to make the journey worthwhile.

map_keywords: São Francisco de Assis Church Pampulha

visits.text: Arrive in Belo Horizonte, capital of Minas Gerais founded in 1897. Transfer from Confins airport (40 km, 45 min). Drop your bags, freshen up, and walk to Praça da Liberdade. A good warm-up stroll.

Praça da Liberdade, the historic administrative heart, brings together a dozen early 20th-century buildings now converted into themed museums (Mineiro, Memory, Sciences). The ensemble is known as Circuito Cultural Praça da Liberdade, one of the densest cultural circuits in Latin America.

Drive to Pampulha, 15 minutes north. This modernist complex was commissioned in 1940 by the young mayor Juscelino Kubitschek from a team of artists, most at the start of their careers (Niemeyer, Burle Marx, Ceschiatti), with the notable exception of Cândido Portinari, already recognized as Brazil's greatest living painter after the 1939 New York World's Fair. UNESCO-listed in 2016.

Don't miss the Igreja de São Francisco de Assis at Pampulha. Vaulted reinforced concrete roof, azulejos painted by Portinari on the rear facade, interior decorated with a monumental Stations of the Cross. The bishopric refused to consecrate it for 14 years, deeming it "too modern." Open for worship since 1959.

activities.text: Dine at Mercado Central, the gastronomic soul of Belo Horizonte since 1929. Over 400 stalls, cachaça counters, mineira charcuterie, artisanal cheeses. Order the classic: pão de queijo + fígado com jiló (liver with jiló, a local bitter vegetable) + Boazinha cachaça. Lively, crowded atmosphere.

practical_info.tip: Avoid rush hours (noon-2pm), prefer 5pm-7pm

activities.text: If the evening stretches on, head up to Mirante Mangabeiras for panoramic views of the illuminated city. The Belvedere neighborhood also offers excellent rooftops.

map_keywords: São Francisco de Assis Church Ouro Preto
Nossa Senhora do Pilar Church Ouro Preto
Nossa Senhora do Rosário dos Pretos Church

visits.text: Drive from Belo Horizonte to Ouro Preto via BR-356 (100 km, 1h45). The last 20 kilometers climb into the Serra do Espinhaço. Park at the Estação parking lot at the bottom of town. The center is pedestrianized and very steep: proper shoes essential.

Start at Praça Tiradentes, Ouro Preto's beating heart. It's dominated by the former municipal palace and prison of 1784, now the Museu da Inconfidência. The museum chronicles the 1789 conspiracy and houses the conspirators' pantheon in its crypt. Tiradentes, the movement's central figure, was hanged then quartered in Rio de Janeiro on April 21, 1792; his head was subsequently displayed on this square as a warning.

The Igreja de São Francisco de Assis, commissioned in 1766, is the absolute masterpiece of Antônio Francisco Lisboa, known as Aleijadinho. The rococo facade, soapstone medallions, and carved pulpits bear his signature. The ceiling painted by Mestre Ataíde depicts a mulatto Virgin surrounded by musician angels, an iconic work of mineiro baroque.

The Igreja Matriz de Nossa Senhora do Pilar, steps away, presents Brazil's most opulent colonial interior. Over 400 kilos of gold leaf cover altars, columns, and galleries. Red velvet curtains at the side balconies create a striking theatrical effect. The country's second-most gilded church after São Francisco in Salvador.

The Igreja de Nossa Senhora do Rosário dos Pretos, built in the 18th century by and for freed African slaves, breaks with conventional rectangular plans. Its unique rounded form and curved lines herald a popular baroque—less gilded but profoundly alive.

activities.text: Lunch on comida mineira at a pay-by-weight restaurant downtown. Tutu de feijão (bean purée with manioc flour), frango com quiabo (chicken with okra), couve (shredded collard greens), unlimited pão de queijo. Expect 50-70 BRL per person with drinks.

Afternoon: lose yourself in the steep cobbled lanes. Every turn reveals a new perspective: twin bell towers framed by green hills, colonial facades with wrought-iron balconies, plunging views over red-tiled roofs. Ouro Preto savors best on foot, slowly.

Late afternoon aperitif facing Igreja São Francisco with an artisanal mineira cachaça (Boazinha, Salinas, Havana). Ouro Preto is a lively university town: the repúblicas (student houses inspired by Coimbra) give weekend evenings a festive atmosphere.

visits.text: Second day based in Ouro Preto with an excursion to Mariana, 14 km away. Leave early to visit Mina da Passagem in the morning, halfway between the two towns. It's Brazil's largest gold mine open to visitors. Worked from 1719 to 1985, it produced 35 tons of gold.

A funicular trolley descends 120 meters underground into galleries carved in the 18th century by enslaved people. At the bottom, a translucent underground lake allows swimming. Guided 45-minute tour, equipment included. A striking immersion into the brutal reality of the gold cycle.

Mariana, founded in 1696, was Minas Gerais's first planned capital and the seat of the first mining bishopric. Its grid plan laid out in 1745 by military engineer José Fernandes Alpoim is unique in the region. Its main streets (Rua Direita, Dom Viçoso, Dom Silvério) can be walked in two hours.

The Catedral Basílica da Sé de Mariana preserves its German Arp Schnitger organ, gifted by the Portuguese crown in 1753—one of the rare instruments by this genius craftsman still in working order worldwide. Concerts on Fridays at 11:30am and Sundays at 12:15pm. The interior is stunningly rich, covered in gold.

Praça Minas Gerais forms one of Brazil's finest baroque urban ensembles. The Igrejas São Francisco and Nossa Senhora do Carmo, near-twins, stand in perpendicular tension around the Pelourinho (pillory) and the Casa de Câmara e Cadeia (1798). Inside Igreja São Francisco: paintings by Mestre Ataíde (who is buried there), soapstone pulpits attributed to Aleijadinho.

activities.text: Lunch in Mariana on Rua Dom Viçoso. Restaurante Lua Cheia serves pay-by-weight mineira cuisine in panelas de pedra-sabão (soapstone pots). Specialties: feijão tropeiro, frango caipira, sorvete de queijo com goiabada for dessert. Good value.

Return to Ouro Preto late afternoon. Pleasant option: the Trem da Vale connects the two towns Friday-Sunday in panoramic carriages (1h journey, 85 BRL round-trip). Online booking required. Departure times vary by season, check schedules.

practical_info.tip: Friday-Sunday and holidays only

activities.text: Dinner in Ouro Preto. Try Bené da Flauta or Chafariz: revisited mineira cuisine with city views. Prices somewhat above average (90-120 BRL per person), but quality delivers. Weekend reservations recommended.

map_keywords: Bom Jesus de Matosinhos Sanctuary Congonhas

visits.text: Drive from Ouro Preto to Congonhas via BR-040 (85 km, 1h50). Congonhas is a fairly ordinary modern mining town, but it shelters one of the twin summits of world baroque: the Santuário Bom Jesus de Matosinhos. Park at the sanctuary lot.

The Santuário Bom Jesus de Matosinhos, UNESCO-listed in 1985, dominates the city from atop Monte Maranhão. Founded in 1757 by Portuguese settler Feliciano Mendes as payment for a healing vow, it brings together a basilica and six Passion chapels. You climb a monumental ramp flanked by six chapels and then encounter the Prophets.

The twelve Prophets, carved in soapstone between 1800 and 1805 by an Aleijadinho already ravaged by disease, form the absolute pinnacle of his work. Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, Daniel, and eight others answer each other from a distance in theatrical poses, hands raised, scrolls unfurled—designed to be "read" from the bottom of the forecourt.

In the six Passion chapels, 66 life-size polychrome cedar sculptures, created by Aleijadinho and his apprentices between 1796 and 1799, depict Christ's Passion. The polychromy, executed by Mestre Ataíde, gives each face a striking intensity. One of the world's most complete sacred sculptural ensembles.

Take the road to Tiradentes, 170 km southwest via BR-265 (2h45). Arrive late afternoon. Leave the car at the town entrance parking: the center is strictly pedestrian. Drop your bags at the pousada and set out to observe the village in the low-angled light.

activities.text: Lunch in Congonhas before departing. The town has no great gastronomic tradition, but Restaurante da Praça facing the sanctuary serves a decent mineiro buffet at 45 BRL. Quick and efficient.

Dinner at Largo das Forras, Tiradentes' central square and nightlife hub. Contemporary mineira cuisine restaurants (Tragaluz, Theobroma, Uai d'Italia). Book ahead on weekends: Tiradentes is one of Brazil's major gastronomic destinations.

practical_info.booking: Required on weekends and during festivals

map_keywords: Pilar Basilica Cathedral São João del Rei
São Francisco de Assis Church São João del Rei

visits.text: Morning in Tiradentes. Begin at the Igreja Matriz de Santo Antônio on the heights. Built from 1710, it's considered one of mineiro baroque's masterpieces. The design (risco) of its rococo facade was commissioned from Aleijadinho in 1810, but execution was carried out by other craftsmen after his death in 1814. The interior is one of colonial Brazil's most richly gilded, with altarpieces and pulpits covered in gold leaf.

The Portuguese organ preserved inside, installed in the late 18th century and still functional, is one of Brazil's rare baroque instruments. Regular concerts (Friday, Sunday). The church also preserves ceiling paintings attributed to Antônio de Caldas, rare representations of baroque allegorical scenes.

Descend to the Chafariz de São José, built in 1749. It supplied the town with drinking water via a one-kilometer aqueduct built by enslaved laborers. Three distinct basins: washerwomen, horse trough, running water for the population. The ensemble still functions today.

Allow about 1h to drive Tiradentes → São João del-Rei (12 km) and board the Maria Fumaça for the return journey to Tiradentes. Departures generally leave São João del-Rei at 10am and 1:30pm, returns from Tiradentes at 11am and 2:30pm (Friday-Sunday). The 762mm narrow-gauge line, inaugurated in 1881 in the presence of Dom Pedro II, is Brazil's oldest Maria Fumaça still in operation. Pulled by period steam locomotives. 12 km journey in 35-40 min.

São João del-Rei, population 90,000, is the commercial and university town complementing Tiradentes. Visit the Catedral Basílica Nossa Senhora do Pilar (1721) on the main square. The church preserves a late 18th-century baroque organ and two bells cast in Portugal that give the city its nickname "terra onde os sinos falam" (land where the bells speak).

The Igreja São Francisco de Assis (1774), near the humpback bridge, has a unique curvilinear facade attributed to Aleijadinho's school. Inside rests Tancredo Neves, president-elect in 1985 who died before his inauguration. The neighboring Memorial Tancredo Neves chronicles Brazil's 1985 democratic transition.

activities.text: Lunch in São João del-Rei. Restaurante Churrascaria Ramon serves a solid meat rodízio at a gentle price (70 BRL). For a more typical option, Quinto do Ouro offers traditional mineira cuisine in a vaulted room.

Return to Tiradentes late afternoon. If time permits, detour via the Serra de São José, the limestone massif overlooking the town. Short trails (1-3h) to Cachoeira do Mangue and viewpoints. Guide recommended (50 BRL): trails are poorly marked.

Evening in Tiradentes. Sunset aperitif on a terrace at Largo das Forras, then gastronomic dinner. Tragaluz (contemporary mineira cuisine) and Pousada Thymus (artisanal cachaça cellar) are two benchmarks. Calm, romantic town in the evening.

visits.text: Long driving day. Count on 5h30 behind the wheel for 395 km via Belo Horizonte (BR-040 then BR-135). Early departure recommended: 7am from Tiradentes to arrive in Diamantina mid-afternoon with time for a first exploration. Lunch stop in Belo Horizonte (Restaurante Xapuri or Mercado Central).

Diamantina, perched at 1,114m altitude in the Serra do Espinhaço, was UNESCO-listed in 1999 as a "remarkable example of human endeavor in a hostile environment." Founded in the 18th century as Arraial do Tijuco, it was the world center of diamond extraction under the Portuguese Crown. Entirely pedestrianized historic center.

First stroll through the steeply cobbled streets. White houses with colorful balconies, muxarabis (latticed balconies inherited from Maghrebi influence), and lajes de pedra pavements create a uniquely Brazilian urban landscape. Elevation varies 150 meters across the historic center alone.

Don't miss the Passadiço da Glória, a small blue covered bridge spanning the street and connecting two historic casarões. Now the symbol of UNESCO candidacy, it houses UFMG's geology center. Photo essential.

The Casa de Chica da Silva, Praça Lobo de Mesquita, is the former residence (1763-1771) of Francisca da Silva de Oliveira. Born enslaved around 1732, freed in 1753 by her master and companion, the immensely wealthy diamond contractor João Fernandes de Oliveira, she subsequently led a lady's life: she had her own church for Black people built, bore 13 recognized children, received a refined education. Now a museum.

activities.text: The Mercado Municipal, known as Mercado Velho, on Praça Barão do Guaicuí, is one of Diamantina's postcards. Wooden structure from 1835 initially built as a tropeiros (muleteers) inn. Its arches reportedly inspired Oscar Niemeyer for the Palácio da Alvorada in Brasília. Market on Friday and Saturday mornings.

On Thursday, Friday, and Saturday evenings, the Vesperata takes place—a unique musical tradition. Musicians play from the balconies of colonial houses overlooking Rua da Quitanda while the audience dines at tables set up below. A magical moment typical of the town. Reserve a table in advance.

practical_info.tip: Rua da Quitanda restaurant reservation required

map_keywords: Nossa Senhora do Rosário Church Diamantina
São Francisco de Assis Church Diamantina

visits.text: Full day in Diamantina and surroundings. Begin at the Igreja de Nossa Senhora do Rosário, the town's oldest church, built between 1728 and 1731 by the confraternity of freed African slaves. Sober interior, painted choir ceiling, bell that has withstood three centuries of weather. Unique tower on the right side, rare in the region.

The Igreja de São Francisco de Assis, located on Praça Juscelino Kubitschek at slightly higher elevation, preserves in its nave the presumed burial place of Chica da Silva. Interior decorated with illusionist trompe-l'oeil paintings by José Soares de Araújo, rare in the region's mineiro baroque.

The Casa de Juscelino Kubitschek, Rua São Francisco 241, is the birthplace of the president who built Brasília (1956-1960). Traditional pau-a-pique house where he lived until age 18. The museum preserves personal objects, photographs, and correspondence. JK never stopped loving his hometown: he had a hotel, school, and faculty built there by Oscar Niemeyer.

The Museu do Diamante, housed in the Casa do Padre Rolim (one of the 1789 conspirators), chronicles four centuries of diamond extraction. Tools, measuring instruments, rough diamond collection, but also sacred art pieces and torture instruments used on enslaved miners. A raw testimony of colonial Brazil.

After lunch, excursion to Biribiri, 14 km north via a dirt road. Former textile industrial village founded in 1876, preserved in original condition. Waterfalls and natural pools on the Rio Ribeirão Pequeno. The Igrejinha de São João Batista (1876), a tiny pink chapel, is the site's postcard.

activities.text: Lunch at a mineira trattoria in downtown Diamantina. Recanto do Antônio or Tragaluz Diamantina serve iconic dishes at fair prices. The typical jequitinhonha: arinto wood with sertão vegetables. Count 50-80 BRL.

Afternoon swimming in Biribiri's waterfalls. Multiple access points: Cachoeira dos Cristais, Cachoeira Sentinela, Cachoeira da Toca. Cool water (20°C even in summer), natural pools with polished rocks. Guide recommended from Diamantina (tour 150 BRL/person).

practical_info.tip: Closed shoes essential, sunscreen

activities.text: Last evening in Diamantina. If you haven't yet, enjoy the Vesperata or a simple dinner at the historic center's terrace tables. The local cachaça, Havana de Salinas, is produced in the Vale do Jequitinhonha 200 km north.

visits.text: Drive Diamantina → Belo Horizonte via BR-040 (295 km, 4h30). Breakfast stop in Curvelo. Arrive in Belo Horizonte early afternoon. Depending on your flight time, two options: drop bags at airport luggage storage or head straight to the final stop.

Late flight option: detour to Inhotim, an open-air contemporary art institute founded in 2006 by businessman Bernardo Paz. 140 hectares of botanical gardens (Roberto Burle Marx), 23 galleries dedicated to major artists (Hélio Oiticica, Cildo Meireles, Tunga, Matthew Barney). The world's largest open-air contemporary art museum.

If departing early evening, simply spend the afternoon in downtown Belo Horizonte. Rua Sapucaí in the Floresta neighborhood for street art, Praça da Liberdade for museums you missed on D1, Mirante do Mangabeiras for a final view.

activities.text: Lunch at Inhotim at one of its three restaurants (Oiticica, Bistrô, Tamboril). Contemporary cuisine, mineiro terroir ingredients. Count 80-120 BRL per person. Weekend reservations recommended.

Final pão de queijo of the trip at Confins airport. The Casa do Pão de Queijo chain has a presence there—you can bring frozen ones home in carry-on (insulated bags sold on-site). True enthusiasts complete with a bottle of artisanal cachaça at duty-free.

practical_info.tip: Frozen pão de queijo: carry-on only, <24h travel time

seo.h1_title: Historic Cities of Minas Gerais - Colonial Gold Circle
seo.intro_paragraph: Eight days in the heart of Brazil's mining state, tracing Aleijadinho's legacy and the 18th-century gold rush. Ouro Preto and Mariana, cradles of mineiro baroque, Congonhas and its UNESCO Twelve Prophets, Tiradentes and its richly gilded Igreja Matriz de Santo Antônio, and finally Diamantina, diamond capital, birthplace of Juscelino Kubitschek and home of Chica da Silva. A concentrated journey through architecture, colonial history, and mineira gastronomy.
seo.seo_hook: Three UNESCO sites, Aleijadinho's Twelve Prophets, 400 kg of gold at Nossa Senhora do Pilar, the Maria Fumaça steam train, and Diamantina's Vesperata in an 8-day loop from Belo Horizonte.
featured_list: Ouro Preto (UNESCO 1980): former Vila Rica, capital of Brazilian baroque and birthplace of independence in 1789
Igreja São Francisco de Assis in Ouro Preto: Aleijadinho's absolute masterpiece, ceiling painted by Mestre Ataíde
Mariana: first planned capital of Minas Gerais (1696), Praça Minas Gerais with unique urban baroque ensemble
Mina da Passagem: Brazil's largest gold mine open to the public, descent 120 meters underground
Santuário Bom Jesus de Matosinhos in Congonhas (UNESCO 1985): the Twelve Prophets carved by Aleijadinho
Tiradentes: Igreja Matriz de Santo Antônio, richly gilded interior and facade designed by Aleijadinho in 1810
Maria Fumaça: 1881 steam train connecting Tiradentes to São João del-Rei in 35 minutes
Diamantina (UNESCO 1999): colonial diamond capital, home of Juscelino Kubitschek and musical Vesperata tradition

itineraries.title: Southern Pantanal & Bonito - Wetlands, Jaguars and Crystal Rivers
itineraries.subtitle: Eight days in Brazil's center-west, between the world's largest tropical wetland and Bonito's turquoise rivers, the global capital of freshwater snorkeling.
itineraries.notes: Arrival and departure via Campo Grande (direct flights from GRU/São Paulo, GIG/Rio and BSB/Brasília). Bonito 300 km away (4.5 hours via BR-060 then MS-382), southern Pantanal 220 km (Estrada Parque from Miranda). Dry season May-October ideal: passable roads, wildlife concentrated around water points. Activities in Bonito are strictly quota-controlled (daily quota per site): booking 2-3 weeks ahead mandatory, via a certified local agency. Bring swimsuit, good closed shoes for floats, binoculars and strong mosquito repellent.

visits.text: Arrival in Campo Grande, capital of Mato Grosso do Sul, nicknamed Cidade Morena for its characteristic red earth. Flight approximately 2 hours from São Paulo. Drop your bags at your downtown hotel and spend the afternoon getting your first impressions before tomorrow's big push to Bonito.

Founded in 1899, Campo Grande now counts 900,000 inhabitants. It's the agricultural crossroads of Brazil's center-west (soy, cattle, sugar). The cuisine blends Paraguayan, Bolivian and Japanese influences—the largest Okinawan diaspora outside Japan settled here starting in the 1910s.

The Parque das Nações Indígenas, in the city's north, is a vast 119-hectare green space laid out in 1997. Artificial lakes, running track, the Mato Grosso do Sul Contemporary Art Museum (MARCO), and the Memorial da Cultura Indígena dedicated to the state's 10 indigenous peoples (Terena, Kadiwéu, Kaiowá, Guarani, etc.). Free admission.

The Morada dos Baís, a restored 1913 colonial house downtown, contains a small historical museum and café-brewery. It's an excellent starting point for understanding the pioneer history of Mato Grosso do Sul, which separated from Mato Grosso only in 1977. Free admission.

activities.text: Dinner at the Feira Central, one of Brazil's most original dining experiences. Founded in 1929, it brings together a hundred stalls where Okinawan Japanese and Paraguayans have created a unique fusion cuisine. Order the sobá (noodles in clear broth with pork and spring onion), the emblematic specialty. Family atmosphere, very gentle prices.

practical_info.tip: Open Wednesday to Sunday, 5pm-midnight

activities.text: After dinner, a digestif at one of the bars on Rua 14 de Julho. You'll rub shoulders with people from Paraná, Rio Grande do Sul and Bolivia on the same street. Great spot to try an ice-cold tereré (the cold version of mate, served with lime juice). Early to bed—you've got an early drive tomorrow.

visits.text: Early departure Campo Grande → Bonito. 300 km via BR-060, MS-382 and MS-178. Entirely paved but minimum 4.5-hour drive with breaks. Open cerrado landscapes and pastures. Drop your bags at the pousada early afternoon. Warm-up afternoon to get your bearings.

Bonito, population 22,000, is the world capital of river ecotourism. The secret of its ultra-transparent waters lies in the geology: the Serra da Bodoquena is composed of limestone rocks that precipitate all suspended matter onto the riverbeds. Result: underwater visibility exceeding 50 meters in places.

The Gruta do Lago Azul, 19 km from Bonito, is the town's postcard image. Limestone cavity discovered in 1924, 100 m vertical depth. Descend the 200 steps carved into the rock to reach an underground lake with an intensely rare cobalt-blue hue. Prehistoric skeletons of sabre-toothed tigers were found here in 1990.

Return to Bonito late afternoon. Stroll along Rua Coronel Pilad Rebuá, the pedestrianized main artery lined with restaurants and tour agencies. Check your excursion schedule with your partner agency: vouchers are mandatory for all sites, strictly limited by daily quotas.

If time permits, swim at the Balneário Municipal on the Rio Formoso, 7 km from downtown. Developed natural pool, turquoise water populated by piraputangas (silvery fish emblematic of Bonito). Family facility with restaurant, playground, kayak rental. Admission 40 BRL.

activities.text: Dinner at a downtown restaurant. Casa do João or Juanita are solid bets. Pantaneira cuisine: grilled pintado (catfish), breaded traíra, banana farofa, palm heart salad. Local wine or Pacific beer. Figure 70-100 BRL.
Early to bed—6am wake-up tomorrow for the Rio Sucuri float. Activity departures in Bonito are strictly scheduled (7am, 9am, 11am, 1pm, 3pm slots) to preserve the environment. No delays tolerated.

practical_info.tip: Check exact voucher time the night before

visits.text: First float: Rio Sucuri, 19 km from Bonito (Fazenda São Geraldo). After briefing and wetsuit fitting, short forest walk to the springs bubbling up from the ground (fervedouros). Mask and snorkel on, inflatable vest mandatory, and you drift 1,800 m on the slow current.

The Rio Sucuri is the third-most crystalline waterway in the world. Its name comes from its serpentine meanders ('sucuri' = anaconda, rare in the tourist zone). 2h20 float with support boat, observing silvery piraputangas, dourados, curimbas and emerald underwater vegetation. Depth 2-3 m. Touching the bottom is forbidden.

Lunch on site at Fazenda São Geraldo. Rustic but generous buffet, local products, dessert of doce de leite beaten in copper kettles. Figure 60-80 BRL with drink. Essential break before the afternoon activity.

Afternoon: Aquário Natural, 7 km from Bonito, in the Reserva Ecológica Baía Bonita. Short float (40 min) but spectacular in an area where piraputangas approach within 10 cm of your face. Ideal for families with children (from age 4) and for a first-time experience of the discipline.

Alternative for the more athletic: long float at Rio da Prata, 53 km from Bonito (in Jardim MS). 50-minute walk through the riparian forest (tapir, armadillo, birds) then 2,200 m float on the Olho d'Água and Prata rivers. The greatest number of visible fish of Bonito's four floats: piraperas, pintados, dourados.

activities.text: Return to Bonito late afternoon. Recovery session at the pousada (hammock, pool). Quiet evening. Many pousadas organize a gathering around a campfire with grilled pantaneira cuisine (churrasco style). Book the day before, 80-100 BRL per person.

For insomniacs, brief observatory escape: Bonito's sky, free of light pollution, lets you see the southern Milky Way with the naked eye. The Southern Cross and Alpha Centauri are easily identified. April-September is the best period for observation.

visits.text: Second day of activities in Bonito with several options depending on your preference. The must-do: Cachoeira Boca da Onça in Bodoquena, 56 km from Bonito. 156 m drop, the highest in Mato Grosso do Sul. 3 km trail, 800 steps, 10 other waterfalls along the route, swimming pools.

The complete Boca da Onça circuit takes 6-7 hours round trip (250 BRL with lunch). Trail with 180 m elevation gain. Add the optional rappel down the waterfall (90 m zipline descent, 150 BRL extra) for the more daring. Panoramic view over the Rio Salobra valley.

More athletic alternative: Abismo Anhumas. Vertical chasm 72 m deep accessible by rappel (15-min descent, 30-min mechanical winch ascent). At the bottom, underground lake housing 19.5 m tall calcite cones, the tallest known in the world. Optional float in the crystalline waters (adds 1 hour).

Less demanding option: Estância Mimosa, 24 km from Bonito. 4 km trail, 8 successive waterfalls in a canyon, swimming in turquoise pools. Family atmosphere, constant shade, less crowded than Boca da Onça. 220 BRL with lunch.

On the way back, detour via Projeto Jiboia. Educational center run by biologist Henrique Nicolete dedicated to cerrado snakes (boas, jiboias, jararacas). Demystifies local reptiles, supervised handling of a non-venomous constrictor, very educational and accessible to children.

activities.text: Lunch at your chosen activity (included in most vouchers). Traditional pantaneira cuisine cooked over wood fire, arroz carreteiro (rice with dried meat), farofa, beans, free-range chicken. Products from surrounding fazendas.

Last dinner in Bonito. Try Vicio da Gula for revisited regional cuisine or Taboa Bar for cocktails with local cachaça. Final evening: pack a light bag for the Pantanal (3 days), main luggage stays in Campo Grande or Miranda depending on the agency.

visits.text: Transfer Bonito → southern Pantanal. 220 km, 4-hour drive. The last 100 kilometers follow the Estrada Parque (MS-184), a 120 km dirt road parallel to the Rio Negro. Agencies generally offer transfers from Bonito or Miranda. Arrival at the fazenda early afternoon.

The Pantanal is the world's largest tropical wetland: 210,000 km² spread across Brazil (80%), Bolivia and Paraguay. UNESCO-recognized since 2000. Between May and October, the wet-season waters gradually recede, concentrating wildlife around the remaining water points. An open-air wildlife-watching paradise.

Welcome lunch at the fazenda. Traditional fazendas (Caiman, Aguapé, Barranco Alto, San Francisco) are former ranches converted into eco-lodges. Simple rooms or suites depending on category, full board included, 2-3 daily excursions with bilingual naturalist guides.

First outing in open 4x4 at sunset. The southern Transpantaneira or the fazenda's tracks reveal capybaras in groups (the world's largest rodent), jacaré caimans (population of 10 million across the entire Pantanal), marsh deer, mazama deer. Sunset is magical: orange light, silhouettes of cuba trees, toco toucan calls.

activities.text: Dinner at the fazenda. Pantaneira cuisine: arroz com pequi (rice with the emblematic cerrado fruit), grilled pacu fish, garden salads, doces de goiabada-com-queijo (guava jam with cheese). Dinner is communal, excellent opportunity to chat with other guests and guides.

Optional night safari after dinner (9pm-10pm). Open 4x4 equipped with powerful headlights. Chance to observe nocturnal wildlife: giant anteaters, armadillos, pacas, porcupines, sometimes ocelot or tapir. Caiman eyes shine in the light, by the dozens. Supplement 80-120 BRL per person.

practical_info.tip: Strong insect repellent mandatory, long clothing

visits.text: Full-day safari in three complementary formats: boat in the morning, guided forest walk midday, horseback or 4x4 at dusk. 5:30am wake-up, hearty breakfast (fruits, eggs, tapioca, pão de queijo) then 6:30am departure to enjoy the morning cool.

Boat safari on the Rio Negro or one of the corixos (water channels). 3 hours of observation from the water, silent motor, flat-bottom boat. Passing under flowering yellow and pink ipê trees, observing aracuã (noisy bird), green kingfishers, white herons, diving cormorants. If lucky, the famous pirarucu, a 2 m prehistoric fish.

With great luck, jaguar observation on a riverbank. The Pantanal harbors the world's largest population and biggest individuals (up to 140 kg). The central and northern zones (Porto Jofre) offer the best probabilities (95% in high season). In the south, observation remains possible but more random (30-40%).

Return to the fazenda for a late lunch and mandatory siesta (heat at its peak). Plunge in the natural pool. Passive observation from the hammocks: you regularly cross paths with capybaras nearby, toucans in the trees, and sometimes a gato-do-mato (ocelot) late afternoon.

Late afternoon horseback outing, quintessential pantaneira tradition. The pantaneiros (Pantanal gauchos) have been raising cattle in these plains since the 18th century. Peaceful 2-hour ride through flooded pastures, crossing corixos swimming. Accessible even to beginners.

activities.text: Observation of hyacinth macaws (ararauna) late in the day. The world's largest parrot (1 m wingspan), deep cobalt blue plumage, nests in the hollows of manduvi palms. The Refúgio Caiman fazenda hosts the Projeto Arara Azul which saved the species from extinction (numbers rising from 1,500 in 1990 to 5,500 today).

Second dinner at the fazenda. Pantaneira tradition: costela de javali (wild boar ribs) over wood fire, paçoca de pilão (pounded dried meat paste), tereré (cold mate) as an aperitif. Conversation with the manager and pantaneiros around the fire, jaguar anecdotes, viola caipira singing.

visits.text: Last full day in the Pantanal. Typical program: morning guided walk in gallery forest (accessible from the fazenda), return for lunch, specialized afternoon activity according to your wishes (piranha fishing, long horseback ride, targeted photo safari).

2.5-hour guided walk in the mata-galeria, ecotone between cerrado and flooded zone. The guide explains pantaneira botany: ipê amarelo, aroeira, piuva (the region's emblematic tree, spectacular pink flowering June to August), bocaiuva (palm that nourishes hyacinth macaws). Approach of animal tracks: jaguar, tapir, peccary prints.

Possible excursion to Fazenda San Francisco, 8 km from Miranda, specializing in last-chance night safaris for jaguar. Internationally recognized conservation program since 2003: every cattle loss to jaguars is compensated, eliminating revenge killings. Result: high feline density in the area.

Piranha fishing for enthusiasts. Emblematic Pantanal activity: hand line, red meat bait, in shallow corixos. The piranha caju is most common (40-50 cm maximum). No real danger to the fisherman (accidents are myths), but watch the teeth when handling catches. Grilled evening assured.

Late afternoon: final outing at sunset. The pantaneiros call this moment 'hora mágica'—the sky turns mauve above the palms, caimans climb back onto the banks in groups, migratory birds gather in tight flocks. A suspended moment of grace before departure.

activities.text: Farewell dinner at the fazenda. Some fazendas organize a fogueira (campfire) with the pantaneiros, traditional songs, demonstrations of laços (lasso) and doma (horse breaking). A rum infused with local cachaça and jabuticaba concludes the evening.

Tipping recommended for guides and staff (not mandatory but much appreciated): 50-100 BRL per person for the naturalist guide, 30-50 BRL for the service team, discreetly handed over the morning of departure. Prepare the cash beforehand—no ATMs on site.

practical_info.tip: Bring cash in small bills

visits.text: Return Pantanal → Campo Grande. Early wake-up (6am), full breakfast, farewell to the fazenda. 4x4 transfer to Miranda (45 km via Estrada Parque, 1.5 hours) then paved BR-262 to Campo Grande (175 km, 2.5 hours). Arrival in Campo Grande around noon depending on return flight time.

If late flight (after 4pm), last stop at Mercado Municipal Antônio Valente. Built in 1927, it preserves its neo-colonial architecture and regional product stalls. Mineiro cheeses, cachaças, cerrado spices (pequi, baru, cagaíta), vacuum-packed dried meat (carne-de-sol) for the return. Guarani and Terena craft souvenirs.

The Memorial da Cultura Indígena, in the Marçal de Souza neighborhood, is a circular construction shaped like a Terena maloca (collective indigenous house) designed by architect Sinval Medina. Permanent exhibitions on Mato Grosso do Sul's 8 indigenous ethnicities. Free admission, closed Mondays.

Final lunch in Campo Grande. Casa Colonial serves upscale pantaneira cuisine (pacu in cupuaçu sauce, costela on open fire) from 120 BRL. More economical alternative: return to Feira Central for a last sobá. Then transfer to Antônio João airport (7 km, 15 min).

activities.text: Last-minute shopping at the airport. Terena crafts (typical red pottery), tereré kit (gourd + bombilla), bocaiuva flour, pequi oil cosmetics. Pequi products are emblematic of the cerrado: beware the powerful and very specific taste (taste before buying in quantity).

Flight Campo Grande to GRU/São Paulo (2h) or GIG/Rio (2.5h), international connection. For combined stays, recommended extension: Bonito + Pantanal combines perfectly with Rio de Janeiro or with Iguaçu (2-hour flight from Campo Grande).

seo.h1_title: Southern Pantanal & Bonito - Wetlands, Jaguars and Crystal Rivers
seo.intro_paragraph: Eight days in Brazil's center-west, in Mato Grosso do Sul, encountering two complementary natural wonders. First Bonito, world capital of freshwater snorkeling: rivers with absolutely translucent waters, cobalt-blue limestone caves and hidden waterfalls of the Serra da Bodoquena. Then the Pantanal, the world's largest tropical wetland: boat and 4x4 safaris in search of jaguars, traditional fazendas, observing capybaras, giant otters, hyacinth macaws and hundreds of bird species.
seo.seo_hook: Rio Sucuri snorkeling, Gruta do Lago Azul, jaguar safaris in the Pantanal, pantaneira fazendas and hyacinth macaws in an 8-day loop from Campo Grande.
featured_list: Pantanal: world's largest tropical wetland (210,000 km²), highest jaguar concentration on the planet
Bonito: Brazil's ecotourism capital, rivers with the world's third-most crystalline waters
Rio Sucuri and Rio da Prata: 1,800 to 2,200 m snorkeling floats, silvery piraputangas within mask reach
Gruta do Lago Azul: limestone cave with cobalt-blue underground lake, 200 steps and 100 m depth
Abismo Anhumas: 72 m chasm accessible by rappel, giant calcite cones and underground lake
Cachoeira Boca da Onça: 156 m drop, tallest waterfall in Mato Grosso do Sul
Pantaneira fazendas: full board, 2-3 daily guided excursions, immersion in Pantanal cowboy life
Hyacinth macaw: world's largest parrot, saved from extinction by Projeto Arara Azul at Refúgio Caiman

places_taglines.BR::campo-grande: Where Okinawan fusion meets the gateway to Brazil's wildest wetland
places_taglines.BR::bonito: Crystal rivers so clear you'll forget you're underwater
places_taglines.BR::pantanal-sul: Where jaguars prowl the banks of the world's greatest wildlife theater

highlights: Pantanal, world's largest tropical wetland (210,000 km²), highest jaguar concentration on the planet
Bonito and Serra da Bodoquena, karst rivers with absolutely translucent waters, Brazil's ecotourism capital
Snorkeling floats in Rio Sucuri or Rio da Prata, world's third-most crystalline waters, with silvery piraputangas at mask distance
Gruta do Lago Azul, limestone cave with cobalt-blue underground lake, Bonito's postcard (200 steps)
Abismo Anhumas, 72 m chasm accessible by rappel, underground lake with 19 m giant calcite cones
Cachoeira Boca da Onça, 156 m drop, tallest waterfall in Mato Grosso do Sul

itineraries.title: Chapada dos Guimarães: 4 Days in Brazil's Smallest Chapada
itineraries.subtitle: Explore red sandstone cliffs, national park waterfalls, and the cerrado caves of Mato Grosso.
itineraries.notes: Dry season (May to September) recommended. Cidade de Pedra and Rio Claro Valley require a 4x4 vehicle AND an accredited guide—book in advance. Itinerary can be combined with the northern Pantanal, 5 hours by road via Poconé. Swimming at the base of Véu de Noiva is prohibited (unstable wall).

map_keywords: Véu de Noiva Waterfall Chapada dos Guimarães
Sant'Ana do Sacramento Church Chapada dos Guimarães

visits.text: Leave Cuiabá early in the morning to reach Chapada dos Guimarães, 65 km away via the MT-251 (Rodovia Emanuel Pinheiro). The road climbs gently through the cerrado for about an hour, passing the famous Portão do Inferno along the way—a viewpoint where the asphalt hugs an impressive cliff edge above the valley. The temperature drops by an average of 5 degrees compared to Cuiabá, and the air becomes drier. Drop your bags in the village center, which consists of a few streets around Praça Dom Wunibaldo, then grab a coffee before heading out to explore.

Véu de Noiva is the park's postcard image, and for good reason: an 86-meter waterfall plunging down the Rio Coxipó into a horseshoe-shaped rocky amphitheater where red macaws nest. The falls were called "Bocaina do Inferno" until 1928, when Archbishop-poet Dom Aquino Correa renamed them in a poem dedicated to the town. Access to the viewpoint is free; from the parking area, it's a 550-meter walk in full sun along an exposed trail. Swimming at the base of the falls is prohibited—the sandstone wall is too unstable. The park closes at 4 PM, with last entry at 3 PM.

Before heading back, make a detour to the Mirante do Centro Geodésico, 7 km from the center via a paved road. The sign claims it marks the "geodesic center of South America," equidistant from the Atlantic and Pacific oceans (1,600 km from each). In reality, the official marker is in Cuiabá on the UFMT campus, and Chapada's is just a leveling benchmark. But the view at 845 meters altitude is well worth the trip: on clear days you can see Cuiabá 30 km away as the crow flies, and the sunsets here are spectacular.

Back in the village, take a look at the Igreja Sant'Ana do Sacramento on Praça Dom Wunibaldo. A thatched chapel was built on this site by the Jesuits in the mid-18th century; after their expulsion, it was replaced in 1779 by the current church, with taipa walls and tiled roof. It's one of the few examples of baroque style in Mato Grosso state, classified as national heritage by IPHAN in the 1950s. Inside, Portuguese azulejos from the colonial era line the lower part of the choir.

activities.text: For lunch, settle into one of the typical restaurants in the center. Mato Grosso cuisine blends river fish, manioc, and cerrado fruits. Try the mojica de pintado (fish stew with manioc) or maria-isabel (rice with dried beef). Several places serve fixed-price buffets for around 50-60 R$ per person. Local craft beers are worth sampling—several microbreweries have opened in recent years.

practical_info.tip: Make reservations on weekends—the center fills up with Cuiabá residents on getaways

activities.text: If you arrive early and it's hot, Balneário Salgadeira is 15 minutes by car on the MT-251 between Cuiabá and Chapada. A waterfall spills into a developed natural pool, with a restaurant and picnic area. It's very busy on weekends with families from Cuiabá, quieter on weekdays. Entrance fee (around 20 R$), changing rooms and showers on site.

practical_info.tip: Avoid Sundays—guaranteed crowds

map_keywords: Cidade de Pedra Chapada dos Guimarães
Crista de Galo Vale do Rio Claro
Poço das Antas Chapada dos Guimarães

visits.text: Cidade de Pedra is only accessible by 4x4 with an accredited guide, so set off in the morning with a local agency booked the day before. Count on 10 km of asphalt on the MT-251 then 18 km of soft sand track—hence the need for traction. From the parking area, a 500-meter trail leads to the viewpoints. You'll discover sandstone walls plunging 250 to 300 meters into the Rio Claro valley, sculpted by wind and rain into shapes of needles, towers, and ruins. Access is limited to 120 people per day. Allow 4 hours on site, including viewpoints.

Along the way, plan several stops. You'll easily spot the nests of red-and-green macaws (Ara chloropterus) that take off in flocks at sunset from the rock walls. The rocks are sandstones from the Furnas and Ponta Grossa formations, deposited in the Devonian period about 400 million years ago, when the entire region lay at the bottom of a shallow sea. You can find fossils of marine invertebrates like brachiopods and trilobites. Local guides know the best photo angles depending on the time of day.

After Cidade de Pedra, descend into the Rio Claro Valley to see from below what you saw from above. Crista de Galo is a curious rock formation: its sandstone layers are vertical when they should be horizontal—a geological collapse likely caused by erosion of the base. The climb to the summit (50 meters of elevation gain over a short distance) offers a 360° panoramic view of the rock walls. Walk carefully—the path is narrow.

activities.text: At the foot of the Crista, the Rio Claro flows between two rock walls in water so clear you can see fish several meters away. Ask your guide for mask and snorkel (often included in the price) and try the flutuação at Poço das Antas and Poço Verde. The current carries you gently along as you pass schools of small freshwater fish typical of the cerrado. The water is warmer than at the waterfalls, a rarity in the chapada.

practical_info.tip: Water rises quickly if rain falls upstream—listen to the guide

activities.text: In the evening, head to Mirante Morro dos Ventos, 5 minutes from the center. As its name suggests, the wind blows hard here, but it's one of the best spots for sunset over the rock walls. Several terrace restaurants take advantage of the view. The praça da igreja in the center sometimes hosts regional music concerts (rasqueado, siriri) on Friday nights.

map_keywords: Kiogo Brado Cave Fazenda Água Fria

visits.text: Head to Fazenda Água Fria, 40-46 km from the center via the MT-251 toward Campo Verde, for the cave circuit. Accredited guide mandatory, last entry at 1 PM, park closes at 5 PM. The round-trip trail runs 7 to 10 km depending on options: the path passes the Ponte de Pedra (a natural sandstone bridge) before reaching Caverna Aroe Jari after 1h-1h20 of walking. Along the way, look for the giant jatobá tree that takes at least five people to encircle.

Aroe Jari means "Dwelling of Souls" in Bororo, the language of the indigenous people who occupied the region before colonization. You enter the largest sandstone cave in Brazil, 1,550 meters long, whose entrance chamber measures over 30 meters wide. Inside, the temperature drops to 21°C—refreshing after the walk in the sun. The Salão do Chuveiro, an underground stream, and the Salão Dourado, where the ceiling glows like gold when the lamp illuminates it, are the two highlights. Headlamp mandatory, provided by the guide.

Coming out of Aroe Jari, walk 300 meters and you'll reach the Gruta da Lagoa Azul. A small pool of crystal-clear water reflects light on the wall in intense blue, particularly between 11 AM and 1 PM when the sun penetrates through the opening. Swimming is prohibited to preserve the optical effect: the slightest wave disturbs the coloration. Another 800 meters and you reach Caverna Kiogo Brado ("bird's nest" in Bororo), 273 meters long, fully passable with the guide.

activities.text: The restaurant at Fazenda Água Fria serves a wood-fired regional cuisine buffet, designed to fuel hikers: braised meats, rice with bacon, black beans, grilled manioc, cerrado fruits in compote. Count on 50-70 R$ per person. This is also when guides organize groups for the return: on foot (3 km extra) or by tractor pulling a jardineira (bench cart), much more practical with a full stomach.

practical_info.tip: Opt for the tractor return—you'll need the energy for what's next

activities.text: In the afternoon, on the way back, stop at Cachoeira do Relógio, a few kilometers from the fazenda. This small 5-meter waterfall takes its name from the fact that it's only lit by the sun after noon—hence the "relógio" (clock). The pool is wide and shallow, perfect for ending the day in cool water after the caves. Access is short from the parking area, 5 to 10 minutes of walking.

visits.text: Before leaving Chapada, wake up early for the Circuito das Cachoeiras in the national park. The 6 km trail links seven waterfalls on the Rio Sete de Setembro: Cachoeirinha, Andorinhas, Pulo, Geladeira, Prainha, among others. Most are swimmable, some with developed natural pools. Registration at the visitor center is free but mandatory via the ICMBio website; guide not required. Count on 3 to 4 hours of walking, easy to moderate level.

You descend back to Cuiabá via the MT-251, about 1 hour down. The capital of Mato Grosso, founded in 1719 during the gold rush, deserves a few hours before your flight. Head to the historic center: the Catedral Basílica do Bom Jesus on Praça da República was rebuilt in the 1960s in a controversial style after the demolition of the colonial church. The praça remains the heart of the center.

To understand the region, the Museu Rondon (Museu de Antropologia, on the UFMT campus) displays a remarkable ethnographic collection of indigenous peoples of Mato Grosso: Bororo, Xavante, Karajá, Rikbaktsa. The objects were collected by the Indian Protection Service founded by Marshal Cândido Rondon, himself a Mato Grosso native, starting in 1910. Free admission, closed Mondays.

activities.text: For lunch or a last meal before the airport, the Mercado do Porto, on the banks of the Rio Cuiabá, serves local fish in popular restaurants. Pintado with urucum (orange-red seeds) and roasted pacu are the classics. Count on 80-120 R$ for two. Vendors also offer cerrado fruits and artisanal liqueurs (tucum, baru, cumaru). Noisy and authentic atmosphere—avoid Sunday midday (very crowded).

If you're combining with the northern Pantanal, now's the time to go: Poconé is about 100 km southwest of Cuiabá, the beginning of the Transpantaneira (MT-060), the 145 km dirt road that plunges into the world's largest wetland. Otherwise, Marechal Rondon Airport (Várzea Grande) is 15 minutes from downtown Cuiabá.

seo.h1_title: Chapada dos Guimarães: 4 Days in Brazil's Smallest Chapada
seo.intro_paragraph: Four days to explore the Mato Grosso national park, its red sandstone cliffs, waterfalls along the Rio Coxipó, and Brazil's largest sandstone cave. Single base in Chapada dos Guimarães, one hour from Cuiabá, with a final stop to reach the airport or the Transpantaneira. Itinerary can be combined with a northern Pantanal stay.
seo.seo_hook: Véu de Noiva's 86-meter cascade, Cidade de Pedra by 4x4, Caverna Aroe Jari, and flutuação in the Rio Claro.
featured_list: Véu de Noiva: the national park's postcard image, 86 meters cascading down the Rio Coxipó
Cidade de Pedra: sandstone walls 250 to 300 meters high, 4x4 access mandatory
Caverna Aroe Jari: 1,550 meters, Brazil's largest sandstone cave, meaning Dwelling of Souls in Bororo
Rio Claro Valley: flutuação in natural pools at the foot of red cliffs

places_taglines.BR::chapada-dos-guimaraes-vila: Where colonial Mato Grosso meets sandstone cliffs rising from the cerrado
places_taglines.BR::cuiaba: Gold rush capital founded in 1719, gateway to both chapada and Pantanal

highlights: The national park covers 32,630 hectares of cerrado and sandstone cliffs, created April 12, 1989
Véu de Noiva waterfall drops 86 meters in a vertical stream down the Rio Coxipó, photographed since 1928
Caverna Aroe Jari is Brazil's largest sandstone cave at 1,550 meters long
The region has over 50 archaeological sites with rock paintings and prehistoric fossils

itineraries.title: Chapada dos Veadeiros: 6 Days in the Brazilian Cerrado
itineraries.subtitle: Traverse waterfalls, canyons, and Kalunga communities in a park inscribed on the World Heritage list since 2001.
itineraries.notes: Dry season (May–September) recommended for trails and natural pools. Dirt tracks toward Cavalcante become difficult in rainy season. Car essential, ideally with elevated ground clearance. The national park has been under Parquetur concession since 2019—tickets must be booked online.

visits.text: Leave Brasília early in the morning for the 230-kilometer drive north to Alto Paraíso—about 3.5 hours via BR-020 and then GO-118. The town sits precisely on the 14th parallel south, the same latitude as Machu Picchu, and has lived largely on spiritual tourism since the 1980s. Drop your bags at your pousada in early afternoon: the town center spans just two streets, but this will be your base for the days ahead. Fill up on fuel and withdraw cash as soon as you arrive—gas stations become scarce as you head toward Cavalcante.

Mirante da Janela offers a stunning view down onto the twin waterfalls of Saltos do Rio Preto—80 and 120 meters high, which you'll see up close the following day. The viewpoint sits on private property 8 kilometers from Alto Paraíso, accessible via a dirt track. Count on 2 kilometers of easy walking from the parking area. Go in late afternoon: the golden light hits the waterfall and canyon downstream perfectly. Bring a headlamp for the return if you linger until sunset.

Built around spiritual tourism in the 1980s, Alto Paraíso has kept its village heart. The Saturday morning organic market (if you time it right) offers cerrado fruits: pequi, baru, cagaita. Otherwise, wander the main street and choose one of the restaurants to try galinhada—the chicken-and-rice dish typical of Goiás—or peixe na telha, fish grilled on a clay tile. Several spots also serve vegetarian dishes, a legacy of the alternative community that settled here.

activities.text: To get into the rhythm on your first day, head to Cachoeira dos Cristais. It's 8 kilometers from town center on private property, with an easy 700-meter trail. Quartz crystals scattered across the riverbed give the water a silvery shimmer. The pool is shallow, perfect for a quick dip after the drive. Entry runs around 30 R$. Arrive late afternoon and you'll have the place almost to yourself.

practical_info.tip: Cold water—bring a thick towel

activities.text: Before or after the mirante, settle onto the main street to sample local flavors. Pequi—a yellow, spiny fruit with a distinctive aroma—divides travelers: some love it, others hate it. Try it in arroz com pequi for a gentle introduction. Craft beers from São Bento microbrewery, brewed 8 kilometers from town, are served in several village bars.

map_keywords: Cachoeira Almécegas I Fazenda São Bento
Cachoeira Almécegas II Fazenda São Bento
Vale da Lua Alto Paraíso de Goiás
Jardim de Maytrea Chapada dos Veadeiros

visits.text: Head to Fazenda São Bento, 8 kilometers from Alto Paraíso on GO-239. This private property concentrates three major waterfalls and infrastructure rare in these parts: buffet restaurant, shaded parking, craft microbrewery. Combined entry costs 70 R$. Arrive at opening time (8:30 AM) to enjoy Almécegas I before the heat and tour bus crowds. Keep your ticket—it's valid for all three waterfalls today.

Cachoeira Almécegas I makes you earn it. From the parking lot, count on 1.5 kilometers of steep, rocky descent to a wooden viewpoint, then another 600 meters to reach the pool at the waterfall's base. The cascade drops roughly 45 meters down a quartzite wall, with water that stays ice-cold even in high summer. The return is a real climb—take your time, benches punctuate the ascent. Skip the flip-flops; several passages are slippery.

Jardim de Maytrea stretches along the roadside on the way to São Jorge. It's a wetland dotted with buriti palms—those trees with bright orange fruit you'll see everywhere in the cerrado—serving as a natural water reservoir for the entire region. Fifteen to twenty minutes is enough for the walk. Sunset over the buritis is worth catching. Local legend holds that the name comes from a spiritual vision an inhabitant had in the 1990s.

Vale da Lua isn't like other waterfalls. The Rio São Miguel has spent millions of years carving through carbonate-matrix conglomerate, creating craters, tunnels, and smooth bathtubs that resemble a lunar surface. The rocks range from 500 million to 2.1 billion years old depending on the layer. Count on 1.2 kilometers of easy trail from the parking area, then watch your step on wet stones. Entry is private, around 50 R$. Avoid the rainy season—flash floods are deadly.

activities.text: On the way back toward Fazenda São Bento, take advantage of Poço São Bento. The trail runs 500 meters, flat, accessible to children. The pool is wide and shallow, with an 8-meter waterfall perfect for cooling off after Almécegas I. This is also where the fazenda's annual water polo tournament takes place. Buffet at the pousada right next door for 90 R$—wood-fired comida mineira.

practical_info.tip: Great with kids or for tired feet

activities.text: If you still have energy late afternoon, Fazenda São Bento offers the park's longest zipline, the Voo do Gavião: 850 meters of cable, 100 meters high, at 55 km/h. Two positions available: seated (around 200 R$) or lying down Superman-style (around 250 R$). The activity runs weekends from 9 AM to 4 PM, or weekdays by reservation for groups of six minimum. Otherwise, head back to Alto Paraíso at a leisurely pace and treat yourself to a massage at your pousada—the local specialty.

map_keywords: Visitor Center Chapada dos Veadeiros National Park São Jorge

visits.text: Leave Alto Paraíso for São Jorge, the small gateway village to the national park, 36 kilometers away on GO-239. The streets are red dirt, the pousadas rustic, the vibe hippie-eco. Drop your things and walk five minutes to the visitor center: this is where you buy your park ticket (47 R$ standard rate, book online at Parquetur's site), watch the mandatory orientation video, and sign the waiver. The park opens 8 AM to 6 PM, last entry around noon.

Trilha dos Saltos is the park's grand classic. Roughly 10 to 11 kilometers round trip for a full day out. The viewpoint for Salto 120 comes after 4 kilometers of walking: the waterfall drops in a vertical ribbon 120 meters into a canyon you'll only see from above, the pool being inaccessible. Continue 800 meters to Salto 80, whose wide, deep pool allows swimming. The water is ice-cold, invigorating after the hike. Follow the yellow arrows all the way.

Before heading back, push on to the Corredeiras do Rio Preto. A flat 1.5-kilometer trail from Salto 80 leads to a series of small cascading pools—natural hydromassage where the water runs less cold than elsewhere. A boardwalk accessible to people with reduced mobility was built in 2019. Park guides advise skipping the 12–2 PM portion on the return: with no shade, the sun beats down hard.

activities.text: Evening in São Jorge slows to a complete halt. Go for dinner at Risoteria Santo Cerrado, which serves risottos made with cerrado ingredients (pequi, baru). The village has only about ten restaurants, all within walking distance of each other. No nightclub, no shopping center—just campfires at certain pousadas and an exceptional starry sky thanks to zero light pollution.

practical_info.tip: Reserve your dinner before 7 PM—kitchens close early

activities.text: The park offers visitation noturna Wednesday through Sunday evenings: groups of six maximum, PNCV-accredited guide, headlamps mandatory (phones are not allowed). The sky here, far from cities, reveals the Milky Way to the naked eye. Book in advance at the visitor center—the ticket must be purchased before the ticket office closes.

map_keywords: Cânion I Chapada Veadeiros National Park
Cânion II Chapada Veadeiros National Park

visits.text: Second entry into the park, this time via the trilha vermelha—red arrows. Head for Cânions I and II, two gorges carved by the Rio Preto where water flows between quartzite walls. Cânion II offers a large swimming pool, deep and ice-cold, perfect for a plunge after the approach walk (4 kilometers from the entrance). Cânion I is a bit higher up the trail, with choppier water. Both are on the same loop.

Virtually on the same loop, Cachoeira das Cariocas strings together several small cascades and stepped pools. The trail leading there extends the canyon route; count on 30 minutes of additional walking. This is where you'll encounter the fewest people in the park: groups usually stay at Cânion II. Pack a snack—the spot invites lingering.

On the return, make a detour via Trilha do Cerrado (Seriema), a short, flat 800-meter path through plants typical of the biome: ipê, lobeira, paepalanthus. A local guide will explain, if you've hired one, why the cerrado is one of the world's oldest and most diverse tropical ecosystems, with 350 to 400 vascular plant species per hectare. It's also a good spot for birdwatching late afternoon.

activities.text: To break the rhythm, settle in at Mansa e Brava, an accessible stretch of the Rio São Miguel 10 minutes by car from São Jorge. Pebble beaches, shallow water on the "Mansa" side, playful currents on the "Brava" side. Brazilian families spend the afternoon here with coolers and ice-cold Coke. No entry fee. Ideal for resting your legs after two days of trilhas.

practical_info.tip: No shade—bring a parasol

activities.text: In the evening, ask your pousada to arrange an appointment with a local therapist for a bamboo massage or herbal bath with cerrado plants. Healing practices have been an integral part of Alto Paraíso and São Jorge's identity since the 1990s, inherited from the alternative communities that settled on the fourteenth parallel.

visits.text: The drive to Cavalcante takes 2.5 to 3 hours from São Jorge: loop back through Alto Paraíso, then take GO-118 south to town. The dirt track from Cavalcante toward the Kalunga community of Engenho II adds another 27 kilometers, in fair condition—count on an hour in a standard car, more in rainy season. Fill up on gas in Cavalcante and withdraw cash: Engenho II has neither ATM nor fuel pump, and some merchants don't accept cards.

Engenho II is one of 39 hamlets in Kalunga territory, Brazil's largest quilombola community. The Kalunga descend from enslaved people who escaped Goiás gold mines in the 18th century, took refuge in these isolated valleys, and lived there for three centuries in near obscurity—anthropologist Maria Baiocchi from UFG "rediscovered" them in 1982. The territory covers 262,000 hectares, designated Sítio Histórico e Patrimônio Cultural Kalunga by Goiás State in 1991, and the process for national cultural heritage designation was launched by IPHAN in March 2026. At the CAT (tourist center), you pay entry and hire a mandatory local guide.

The turquoise waters of Cachoeira Santa Bárbara alone explain why you made this entire drive. The waterfall stands 30 meters high, the pool 4 meters deep—learn to swim beforehand or rent a life jacket. The trail runs an easy 1.5 kilometers, after a 4-kilometer transfer in a local pau-de-arara cattle truck (10 R$ round trip). Arrive before 11 AM to catch the sun head-on in the water and capture maximum blue. After 2 PM, shade falls and the color dims.

An 800-meter walk downstream, Cachoeira da Capivara is less crowded but splendid: a cascade over 40 meters into a large, deep pool formed at the confluence of several watercourses. The trail descends steeply for 800 meters with ropes to help. The guide will explain that "Kalunga" comes from Bantu and means "sacred place, place of protection"—an etymology claimed by the community.

activities.text: Have lunch with a local family at Engenho II. Several families open their homes to visitors: everything is cooked over wood fire, most ingredients come from the garden or neighboring fields. Count on 25 to 40 R$ per dish, reserve through your guide early in the morning so they have time to prepare. Ask for the galinhada or grilled fish from the Rio Tocantins. Cerrado fruit juices (cajá, cagaita, pequi) are absolutely worth trying.

practical_info.tip: Reserve through your guide upon arrival at CAT

activities.text: If you have time late afternoon, make a quick stop at Mirante de Nova Aurora, 6 kilometers from Cavalcante toward Engenho II. The panoramic view embraces the entire chapada to the south, and sunsets are spectacular in dry season, when the air is charged with golden dust. No entry fee, no facilities—just the viewpoint and silence.

visits.text: The return drive Cavalcante–Brasília runs about 320 kilometers; count on 4.5 to 5 hours leaving early. You'll loop back through Alto Paraíso then take GO-118 south to Formosa before joining BR-020. If your flight is late afternoon or evening, you'll have time for a half-day in Brasília. Otherwise, spend the night and catch your plane the next day. The federal capital, designed by Lúcio Costa and Oscar Niemeyer, was inaugurated in 1960 and inscribed on the UNESCO list in 1987.

If you have 2–3 hours on-site, head to Praça dos Três Poderes to see the alignment of Palácio do Planalto, Supremo Tribunal Federal, and Congresso Nacional with its two inverted domes. Next, the Catedral Metropolitana, completed by Niemeyer in 1970: its 16 hyperbolic white concrete columns support a roof of stained glass designed by Marianne Peretti. Inside, Alfredo Ceschiatti's suspended angels float in colored light.

Before the airport, make a final stop at Memorial JK to understand how Juscelino Kubitschek brought a capital up from red earth in less than four years, between 1956 and 1960. The president's mausoleum is displayed under a large glass cube, his tomb oriented toward Brasília. Thirty minutes is enough for the express visit. The airport is 15 minutes by car.

activities.text: For a final Brazilian meal before your flight, sit down at a churrascaria in the Asa Sul neighborhood. The rodízio—rotating meat service where you pay a fixed price and servers come to your table with skewers—is an institution. Count on 120 to 180 R$ per person. If you prefer something lighter, the food trucks at Pontão do Lago Sul serve good grilled fish with views over Lake Paranoá.

practical_info.tip: Reserve if you're going at lunch hour—it fills up

activities.text: If your flight is very late and you want one last breath of modern architecture, cross the Ponte JK over Lake Paranoá. Inaugurated in 2002, its three asymmetric arches of 240 meters' span each reflect in the water. It's the postcard image you bring home from Brasília. Small parking at each end for photos.

seo.h1_title: Chapada dos Veadeiros: 6 Days in the Brazilian Cerrado
seo.intro_paragraph: Six days traversing the Goiás cerrado, between the vertiginous waterfalls of the UNESCO-listed national park (inscribed in 2001) and the Kalunga communities of Cavalcante. You'll alternate hiking, ice-cold water swims, and encounters with Brazil's largest quilombola community. Double base in Alto Paraíso then São Jorge, with a final stop in Cavalcante for Cachoeira Santa Bárbara.
seo.seo_hook: 120-meter waterfalls, turquoise waters of Santa Bárbara, rock formations of Vale da Lua, and living Kalunga culture.
featured_list: Alto Paraíso: gateway to the cerrado on the fourteenth parallel south, base for the Almécegas waterfalls
Vale da Lua: conglomerate sculpted by the Rio São Miguel, rocks from 500 million to 2.1 billion years old
São Jorge: rustic village accessing the national park and Trilha dos Saltos (80 and 120 meters)
Cavalcante: colonial gold capital and gateway to the Kalunga Engenho II community

places_taglines.BR::alto-paraiso-de-goias: Spiritual capital of the cerrado, where crystal waters meet the 14th parallel
places_taglines.BR::sao-jorge-vila: Gateway village to Chapada dos Veadeiros National Park's trails
places_taglines.BR::cavalcante: Where 18th-century gold mines border Brazil's largest quilombola territory
places_taglines.BR::brasilia: Niemeyer's modernist capital rising from red earth, UNESCO-listed since 1987

highlights: The national park covers 240,611 hectares of cerrado and was inscribed on the UNESCO list in 2001
Cachoeira Santa Bárbara, in the Kalunga Engenho II community, displays turquoise water over a 4-meter-deep pool
The Saltos do Rio Preto reach 80 and 120 meters, among the highest waterfalls in the region
Kalunga territory is home to Brazil's largest quilombola community, recognized as cultural heritage by IPHAN

itineraries.title: Serra da Capivara: 5 Days Tracking the First Americans
itineraries.subtitle: You'll explore the Americas' greatest rock art ensemble deep in the caatinga, among canyons, millennia-old paintings, and archaeological debates still wide open.
itineraries.notes: Remote region of the Piauí sertão. São Raimundo Nonato airport (NSR) has no regular commercial flights since 2025—access via Petrolina (PE), 303 km / 4.5 hours via BR-235 + BR-324. Guide mandatory for park entry. No rental agencies in São Raimundo, rentals available in Petrolina. Dry season from April to October, intense heat (up to 45°C in September-October). Water, hat, and closed-toe shoes essential.

map_keywords: Museum of the American Man São Raimundo Nonato
Serra da Capivara Airport São Raimundo Nonato

visits.text: São Raimundo Nonato is the mandatory base for exploring the park. This town of roughly 35,000 inhabitants concentrates everything you won't find elsewhere: hotels, banks, supermarkets, restaurants. Since 2025, NSR airport no longer has regular commercial flights—access is via Petrolina (PE), 303 km away, about 4.5 hours along BR-235 then BR-324, now entirely paved. Drop your bags, locate the agency providing your guide, and save the afternoon for the Museu do Homem Americano, which lays the groundwork for everything you'll see next.

The Museu do Homem Americano (FUMDHAM), opened in 1998 in São Raimundo Nonato, displays artifacts from over forty years of excavations: lithic tools, pottery fragments, human remains. This is where the skull from Toca dos Coqueiros is exhibited—it shows distinct characteristics from classic Amerindians and fuels the debate over early American settlement. Allow two hours to cover the entire collection. The foundation, created by Niède Guidon in 1986, manages the park in partnership with ICMBio and IPHAN.

Make use of late afternoon with a visit to the Fábrica de Cerâmica Serra da Capivara in Coronel José Dias, 30 km from São Raimundo. The workshop was founded in 1995 by Niède Guidon as both a social and artistic project: it reproduces the park's rock art motifs on ceramics and provides employment for several dozen local artisans. It's also the only place to buy serious reproductions, made on-site—far superior to market copies. Open Monday through Saturday, reasonable prices.

activities.text: Briefing with your guide (mandatory for park entry) on the next four days' program: choosing circuits according to ability level, packing your bag, water (3 liters minimum per person), closed-toe shoes, hat, flashlight for dark shelters. Ask your guide how to book evening access to Boqueirão da Pedra Furada when the site is lit up.

practical_info.tip: No rental agencies in São Raimundo Nonato—rent your car in Petrolina

activities.text: Dinner on the central praça: carne de sol (dried and grilled meat), baião de dois (rice and beans melded together), grilled queijo coalho, all washed down with cajá or umbu juice—caatinga fruits you won't find anywhere else. Restaurants close around 10 p.m.

practical_info.tip: Ask the locals where to find the best carne de sol—they know

map_keywords: Toca do Inferno Serra da Capivara

visits.text: The Desfiladeiro da Capivara, roughly 40 km from São Raimundo, was the first circuit Niède Guidon explored in the 1970s and remains one of the most visited. First stop: Toca da Entrada do Pajaú, a small rock shelter whose paintings date to around 12,000 years BP. You'll see hunting scenes, dances, daily life, painted in red from iron oxides. The circuit is of medium difficulty—stone paths, stairs, moderate elevation changes.

Toca do Inferno sits in a deep canyon, lacking paintings but boasting a rock cathedral with walls over 50 meters high in sandstone. Local legend claims a mysterious chant rises from the depths at noon—guides enjoy putting you to the test. Further along, Toca da Entrada do Baixão da Vaca offers 749 rock paintings spread across 100 meters of wall, one of the densest panels in the park. Watch out for snakes and bees in the shaded recesses.

To finish the circuit, Toca do Paraguaio was the first site officially catalogued by researchers. Two skeletons were recovered here, dated respectively to 7,000 and 8,670 years BP. The paintings, from the Nordeste tradition, sometimes exceed 1.5 meters in height—rare in the park. Tip: get a guide who'll point out the details (running hunter, copulation scene, animal figures) because some panels are hard to read with the naked eye.

activities.text: Lunch in the povoado of Barreirinho, near the park: local restaurant, comida caseira, galinha caipira, paçoca, mandacaru salad. Many agencies include this in the package. It's also a good time to chat with your guide about the park's oral history—many locals saw Niède Guidon arrive in the 1970s, and their families contributed to the excavations.

practical_info.tip: Try the paçoca de carne seca, sweet-salty, a sertão specialty

activities.text: On the way back, detour through Baixão das Andorinhas in late afternoon to watch the swallows return to the canyon. The spectacle lasts about 30 minutes: thousands of birds pour into the cliff at dusk to roost. Viewing platform provided, easy car access.

map_keywords: Serra Branca Serra da Capivara National Park
João Pimenta Site São Raimundo Nonato

visits.text: Serra Branca is the latest major circuit opened in the park, about 50 km from São Raimundo. Low difficulty, a nice day to rest your legs after yesterday. The olho d'água da Serra Branca, a natural spring, was the only water source for inhabitants before the park's creation in 1979. Several families were relocated to the povoado Novo Zabelê in compensation. The circuit includes sites Toca do Vento, Toca Extrema II, Toca do Veado, and Toca do João Arsena.

Along this circuit, you'll also find remnants of the 'maniçobeiros,' families who extracted latex from the maniçoba (Manihot glaziovii) in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, during the rubber boom period. The local rock paintings, later than those at Desfiladeiro, depict hunting, warfare, and sexuality scenes with remarkable narrative precision. The stylistic tradition called 'Nordeste' has been studied since the 1980s by Anne-Marie Pessis, Guidon's longtime collaborator.

Sítio João Pimenta, 20 km from São Raimundo in the Serra Vermelha region, is a private reserve (RPPN) managed by the descendants of João Pimenta, a former traditional occupant of the area. The trail is 1.5 km round-trip with sandstone formations that have been compared to Turkey's Cappadocia—exaggerated, but the atmosphere is superb. A few rock art sites and, by reservation, rappelling is possible. Visits in small groups only.

activities.text: Visit the povoado of Novo Zabelê, created to resettle families displaced when the park was established in 1979. About a hundred inhabitants now live from beekeeping, cerrado fruit preserves, honey, and handicrafts. The project is partly supported by FUMDHAM. It's a chance to talk with the elders, who still remember the caves their parents frequented long before they became archaeological sites.

practical_info.tip: Buy jandaíra honey and umbu preserves directly from the families

activities.text: For the more adventurous, the Caldeirão dos Rodrigues circuit offers a scalaminhada (half-climbing, half-hiking) on the plateau, passing through Toca do Caldeirão dos Rodrigues—one of the park's finest rock art compositions, dated to around 18,000 years BP according to excavations. Panoramic view of Grotão da Esperança canyon from the summit. Difficult level, plan 4 to 5 hours, bring plenty of water.

map_keywords: Museum of Nature Coronel José Dias
Toca da Pedra Furada Serra da Capivara

visits.text: The Museu da Natureza, opened December 18, 2018 in the municipality of Coronel José Dias, is the park's other major scientific institution. Scenography by Marcello Dantas (creator of the Museu da Língua Portuguesa in São Paulo), 1,700 m² of exhibitions across 12 rooms: birth of the universe, formation of the Earth, cerrado megafauna (giant sloths, mastodons), arrival of humans in the region. The final film, screened in the last room, is narrated by Maria Bethânia. Open Wednesday through Monday from 1 p.m. to 7 p.m., admission around 30 R$.

At the museum exit, the mirante overlooks the park's sandstone cuestas—a breathtaking view, especially in late afternoon when the rock turns orange. A life-size replica of a giant sloth (up to 8 meters tall according to paleontologists) greets visitors on the way out. The project cost 13.7 million reais, financed by BNDES.

Head to Toca da Pedra Furada around midday. No confusion with its namesake in Tocantins: here we're talking about a sandstone formation sculpted by wind into a natural arch, dominating the valley of the same name. The site has essentially lent its name to the park—it's become the iconic image of Serra da Capivara. Short access trail, little elevation gain.

activities.text: In mid-afternoon, set out to visit Boqueirão da Pedra Furada itself, the great rock shelter that made the park known in the scientific world. The cavity measures 70 meters wide by 12 meters maximum depth and dominates the valley by about 20 meters. This is where excavations directed by Niède Guidon between 1978 and 1985, then by Fabio Parenti, uncovered traces of hearths and lithic tools studied in Nature in 1986. The stratigraphic sequence runs 5 meters deep, and the bottom contains dates exceeding 40,000 years BP—still debated in the scientific community.

practical_info.booking: Ask your guide for access to the main shelter
practical_info.tip: Look up: hundreds of painted figures on the wall

activities.text: Dinner and overnight in a pousada in Coronel José Dias or return to São Raimundo depending on your program. Coronel José Dias is tiny (under 5,000 inhabitants) and very quiet—a good option for those who want a night in a more rural setting, near the park, after four intense days.

practical_info.tip: Coronel José Dias = few options, advance booking recommended

map_keywords: Toca da Invenção Serra da Capivara
Casa do Neco Coelho Serra da Capivara

visits.text: The Trilha Hombu—the word means 'come see, come with me' in a local indigenous language according to guides—is one of the most comprehensive circuits to wrap up your visit. It blends prehistoric sites (Toca da Invenção, Toca da Pedra Caída, Tocas da Ema do Sítio do Brás) with recent history: the Casa do Neco Coelho, one of the first families settled in the area before the park's creation, and the small Museu do Neco Coelho which traces the lives of 20th-century residents.

Along this circuit, the guide is often a descendant of the region's historic families—sometimes a son or grandson of the former mateiro guides who accompanied Niède Guidon from the earliest expeditions. The narration thus weaves together scientific archaeology and family memory, lending the route a special character. The circuit includes an option to climb, via a ladder planted in the rock, to the top of the chapadão for a panoramic view.

Before heading back to Petrolina, stop by downtown São Raimundo Nonato for last-minute shopping at the Fábrica de Cerâmica if you didn't make it on D1, or to buy copies of rock paintings directly from the artisans. The town lives largely off the park and the jobs it generates—every purchase counts.

activities.text: Depending on your flight, drive to Petrolina in the afternoon: 303 km, about 4.5 hours on BR-324 then BR-235. The road is now entirely paved, passes through Dirceu Arcoverde, Remanso (BA), Casa Nova, and runs alongside the Rio São Francisco toward the end. Plan to leave no later than 2 p.m. for an evening flight from Petrolina.

practical_info.tip: Fill up in Dirceu Arcoverde, last real gas station before Remanso

activities.text: If you arrive in Petrolina early, detour to the banks of the Rio São Francisco at sunset. The city is twinned with Juazeiro (BA) on the opposite bank and this is one of South America's greatest rivers. A few floating restaurants serve grilled surubim, the river's king fish.

practical_info.tip: Surubim is eaten grilled whole or as breaded fillets

seo.h1_title: Serra da Capivara: 5 Days Tracking the First Americans
seo.intro_paragraph: In southern Piauí, Serra da Capivara National Park concentrates the greatest quantity of rock art sites in the Americas—more than 1,300 recorded sites, 173 open to the public. Inscribed as a UNESCO site in 1991, it challenged classical theories about the peopling of the Americas thanks to Niède Guidon's excavations at Boqueirão da Pedra Furada.
