This 5-day itinerary, based in São Raimundo Nonato, combines archaeological circuits, museums, and the caatinga in full bloom.
seo.seo_hook: The Americas' greatest concentration of rock art, datings that challenge the consensus, two major scientific museums, and caatinga as far as the eye can see.
featured_list: Boqueirão da Pedra Furada: C14 datings published in Nature in 1986, scientific debate still ongoing
Museu do Homem Americano: artifacts from 40 years of excavations directed by Niède Guidon
Museu da Natureza: 12 rooms designed by Marcello Dantas, opened in 2018
Desfiladeiro da Capivara: 749 paintings on Toca do Baixão da Vaca alone, dated to roughly 12,000 years

places_taglines.BR::sao-raimundo-nonato: Where desert stone meets the deepest human past
places_taglines.BR::coronel-jose-dias: Tiny Piauí town guarding the gateway to Earth's memory

highlights: The national park, created in 1979 and inscribed as UNESCO World Heritage on December 13, 1991, covers 129,140 hectares of caatinga across four municipalities in southern Piauí
More than 1,300 archaeological sites recorded, of which roughly 900 contain rock paintings and 173 are open to visitors—the greatest concentration in the Americas

Datings from Boqueirão da Pedra Furada by Niède Guidon (article published in Nature in 1986) challenged the Bering Strait peopling theory around 12,000 years, advancing dates of 32,000 to roughly 48,000 years BP—the hypothesis of over 50,000 years advanced later remains contested and the debate is still open in the scientific community
Niède Guidon (1933-2025), Franco-Brazilian archaeologist, founded FUMDHAM in 1986 and directed research for over 40 years

itineraries.title: Jalapão: 7 Days in Tocantins' Water Desert
itineraries.subtitle: You traverse the cerrado by 4x4 among orange dunes, floating springs and turquoise waterfalls in the heart of central Brazil.
itineraries.notes: Remote region. 4x4 vehicle mandatory, local agency strongly recommended. Dry season May-September only - during rainy season tracks become impassable. Bring plenty of cash, little phone signal. Long distances on sand tracks.

map_keywords: Sussuapara Canyon Ponte Alta do Tocantins
Central square Ponte Alta do Tocantins

visits.text: You arrive in Palmas, capital of Tocantins, and the road heads east. Three hours later, Ponte Alta do Tocantins marks the true gateway to Jalapão: this is where asphalt surrenders to red dirt tracks. The town itself is modest, but its position is strategic, 187 km from Palmas via the TO-030 then the TO-130. Upon arrival, head to Sussuapara Canyon, 15 km away: a narrow fissure between red walls where light barely filters through. Count on 25 minutes of downhill walking.

Sussuapara Canyon descends to 25 meters deep. At the bottom, trickles of water flow from overhanging roots and a small 5-meter jet falls into a natural pool where only one person fits at a time. The sandstone releases a reddish pigment on contact with water - you'll see it beneath your feet. The name comes from the sussuapara, a small deer from the region. Tip: descend around midday, it's the only time when sunlight truly penetrates to the bottom.

Built in 1989 when Tocantins State was carved out from Goiás, Ponte Alta lives up to its name: the town overlooks the Tocantins River. Take a walk through the center, grab a coffee at the rodoviária, watch the pickups equipped for the trail departing in convoy at dawn. It's also your last real opportunity to withdraw cash before heading toward Mateiros - ATMs become scarce inside the park.

activities.text: Briefing with the agency for the coming days. Bag adjustments, 4x4 checks, buying water in quantity (count on 3 liters per person per day minimum), some snacks and dried fruits. Most accommodations inside the park operate without air conditioning and with limited electricity - pack your gear accordingly.

practical_info.tip: Headlamp essential, also bring spare batteries

activities.text: Taste Tocantins cuisine at dinner on the central praça: galinha caipira (slow-cooked free-range chicken), rice pilaf with pequi (a cerrado fruit with a powerful local flavor) and beiju, the tender manioc pancake. Restaurants close early, around 9 PM. It's also time to go to bed early - tomorrow, departure before 8 AM to tackle the 95 km dirt track to Mateiros.

practical_info.tip: Eat pequi avoiding biting the seed (internal spines)

visits.text: Behind the wheel early in the morning, head east on the TO-255 toward Mateiros, the tourist capital of Jalapão. The track is sandy, sometimes fine as flour - hence the 4x4. Before arriving, make the detour to Cachoeira da Velha, the park's largest waterfall: 100 meters wide and about 15 meters high on the Rio Novo. A well-maintained wooden walkway descends to several viewpoints facing the double curtain of water. Swimming is prohibited, the flow being too violent.

Just downstream from Cachoeira da Velha, walk 1.5 km along a forest gallery trail to Prainha do Rio Novo. It's the waterfall's reverse side: calm waters, white sand, small fish visible to the naked eye. Here swimming is not only permitted but practically mandatory after the long track from Ponte Alta. The cachoeira is located on Fazenda Triagro, and a tourist legend widely repeated in the region claims that Pablo Escobar used the location in the 1980s - no official source confirms it, but local guides willingly tell the story along the way.

Mateiros, about 3,000 inhabitants, lives from tourism and cattle ranching. The red-dust grid streets converge on a modest central square. Drop your bags at the pousada in late afternoon, take a walk through town, locate the restaurant where you'll dine (most close around 9 PM). Mateiros will be your base for the next three days - the position is ideal for exploring the dunes, fervedouros and cachoeiras.

activities.text: For thrill-seekers, level-4 rafting starts right at the foot of Cachoeira da Velha and descends the rapids of the Rio Novo to Prainha. It's the angle the walkway doesn't show you. Count on about 2 hours including briefing and suiting up. Advance booking essential with a licensed agency.

practical_info.difficulty: Moderate, must know how to swim

activities.text: Sample regional cuisine at dinner: galinha caipira (free-range chicken) with rice pilaf, paçoca de carne seca (dried beef pounded with manioc flour), beiju (manioc pancake). Many pousadas serve everything under an awning, no menu - you eat what's ready.

practical_info.tip: Prefer pousadas with integrated restaurant, more convenient in the evening

visits.text: Rise before 4 AM. The Serra do Espírito Santo trail begins in darkness: 500 to 800 meters of steep climb on sandstone, sometimes with ropes and benches to catch your breath. The equivalent of 80 floors of a building according to local guides. At the summit, the plateau opens onto a flat cerrado horizon, and just after 6 AM the sun emerges behind the chapadões. It's this serra that, through millennial erosion, provided the sand for the dunes you see below. Headlamp, water, windbreaker essential.

The Jalapão dunes extend over about 38 km² at the foot of the serra. The highest peaks around 30 meters, and the vivid orange color comes from iron oxides in the sand. Park at the base, climb on foot (10 to 20 minutes depending on your pace) and settle in for late afternoon. The park closes at 5:30 PM, mandatory exit before 6:30 PM. Note: no drinks on the dunes, no camping, photos only.

On the TO-255 road toward the dunes, Morro do Saca Trapo rises about 30 km from the center of Mateiros, adjacent to Serra do Espírito Santo. It's an isolated sandstone cone that juts from the flat - a classic photo on the car roof with the morro behind, and many visitors stop there. The nickname comes from a sack of old clothes ("saca de trapo") that old local hunters allegedly left at the foot of the morro. No trail to climb here, you stop at the roadside, look, photograph, and leave. A lanchonete offers drinks before the final stretch to the dunes.

activities.text: Sunset on the dunes is Jalapão's postcard image. The sand gradually shifts from vivid orange to dark red then to purple. Sit on the main crest, stay silent - the winds are strangely calm there, you hear the buritis rustling in the distance. No music, no drones, that's the rule.

practical_info.tip: Stay at least 20 minutes after sunset

activities.text: Between the trail and the dunes, lunch in Mateiros and take a siesta at the pousada - the morning will have knocked you out. Jalapão's rhythm alternates physical efforts and long drives: save your energy when you can.

practical_info.tip: Hammock and fan, the combo that saves the afternoon

visits.text: Head to Cachoeira do Formiga, 35 km south of Mateiros on the TO-110. The pool measures about 8 meters in diameter and 2 meters deep, and the emerald-green water remains stable around 24°C year-round. The waterfall is only 2 meters high but its pressure is powerful: a true natural hydromassage. The bottom sand is white, visibility perfect. Bring a mask or snorkel, you'll see the small fish. Paid entry (about 20 R$), quilombola territory.

Fervedouro do Ceiça, 30 km from Mateiros, is one of the most accessible in the park. The principle is unusual: a water table rises through an opening in the sand, and the water pressure is such that you float effortlessly. No need to know how to swim. The rule is strict: entry in small groups, limited duration to avoid disturbing the source, no sunscreen or repellent (it pollutes immediately).

For comparison, stop by Fervedouro Buritizinho, smaller, surrounded by buriti palms. The sensation is the same but the setting is different - turquoise-blue water, visible roots at the bottom. A few minutes are enough to feel the upward push. These springs are fragile: walk on the walkways, not on the banks.

activities.text: Between two fervedouros, picnic in the shade of the buritis. Many agencies provide a cold meal (sandwich, fruit, cashew juice) included in the passeio. Otherwise, bring your own - no restaurants between sites, just red track.

practical_info.tip: Cooler essential, the sun beats down hard from 11 AM

activities.text: If you still have time in late afternoon, Fervedouro Bela Vista offers a nighttime session with lighting, usually reserved for Pousada Bela Vista guests. Cinema atmosphere, water still at 24°C even at night. Ask your guide about the slot.

visits.text: The povoado of Mumbuca is located 35 km from Mateiros, on the TO-110 road between Mateiros and São Félix do Tocantins. The quilombola community descends from families who arrived from Bahia in the early 20th century - the parents of matriarch Dona Miúda (Guilhermina Pereira Matos, 1928-2010) came to the region around 1909. Today, about 165 inhabitants. The word "mumbuca" refers to a species of stingless bee, very common here, which gave its name to the place. Drop your bags at a local pousada or homestay accommodation.

The Associação Capim Dourado, founded in 2000, is the village's economic heart. About fifty artisans work "capim dourado" there - which is not a grass but a plant called sempre-viva (Syngonanthus nitens), endemic to the cerrado. The fine golden stems are sewn with buriti fiber to make hats, baskets, mandalas, jewelry. According to Dona Miúda's memory, the technique was transmitted in the early 20th century by passing Xerente Indians, who taught the already settled inhabitants to "sew the capim." Buy here directly from the artisans, not from resellers.

Fervedouro do Mumbuca, managed by the community, is one of the oldest to have been opened to tourism. Crystal-clear water, white sand bottom, palms all around. The upward pressure lifts your legs in seconds. The entry fee goes to the association - each visit contributes directly to the community.

activities.text: Participate in a weaving workshop with an artisan: you're shown how to sort the stems, moisten them, sew the first stitches with seda de buriti. Count on about 2 hours to understand the technique. You leave with a small handmade object and the measure of what a hat representing several days of work means.

practical_info.tip: Buy your gifts here, fair prices and direct impact

activities.text: If you're in Jalapão in late August or September, time your visit for the Festa da Colheita do Capim Dourado. Official harvest starts on September 20, preceded by a weekend of songs, dances and viola de buriti. It's the year's highlight for the community - don't photograph anything without asking.

practical_info.tip: Always ask before filming or photographing residents

visits.text: Return west on the same sandy track. Pedra Furada is located about 30 km from Ponte Alta - not the same one as in Mateiros: here too the sandstone was hollowed into a portal by the wind, and late afternoon light filters through the hole like a natural projector. You get out of the 4x4, two minutes' walk through a eucalyptus grove, and you're there. Free visit.

In Pindorama do Tocantins, on the road, Lagoa do Japonês offers a refreshing stop: transparent waters with bluish hues, clear bottom, quieter atmosphere than the star sites. Many make it their last swim before returning to Palmas. Grass beach, a few tables under the trees.

On the final section, heading south before Ponte Alta, you cross capim dourado fields in full vereda - those fine golden stems you saw woven in Mumbuca. Ask your guide to stop for a few minutes on the roadside: backlit, in late afternoon, the vereda becomes literally golden. It's the image you'll keep of Jalapão beyond the dunes.

activities.text: Long 4x4 drive between Mumbuca and Ponte Alta - about 3 hours on track, sometimes more if the road condition has suffered from recent rains. Prepare a long playlist, keep water within reach and accept that half the day will be spent in the pickup. The landscape scrolls by: veredas of buritis, chapadões, sudden watercourses around a bend.

practical_info.tip: Break every 90 minutes, cumulative vibrations are truly tiring

activities.text: Evening in Ponte Alta: choose a simple churrasco on the praça, with grilled picanha and farofa. It's also an opportunity to buy a last kilo of dried capim dourado or a small hat if you didn't have time in Mumbuca.

visits.text: Palmas is Brazil's last entirely planned state capital in the 20th century: foundation stone laid on May 20, 1989, seven months after Tocantins State was created. The avenues are straight, wide, numbered by quadras - a bit like Brasília, cerrado version. Take a tour of Praça dos Girassóis, one of the world's largest urban squares. At the center, the Araguaia Palace houses the state government headquarters.

35 km from downtown Palmas, the Taquaruçu district hides several cachoeiras in the mata. The best known, Escorrega Macaco, descends about 50 meters in two levels and ends in a pool ideal for swimming. Easy trail of about 1.5 km from the parking lot. The name comes from a local climbing plant said to be "slippery as a monkey." If your flight leaves in the evening, it's a perfect last swim.

Praia da Graciosa, on the lake formed by the Lajeado dam on the Tocantins River, is Palmas's urban beach: imported white sand, aligned kiosks, fresh and warm water. It's one of the capital's five official beaches, open year-round. Perfect for finishing the trip gently, after a week of tracks and dust. Local fish restaurants all along the shore, particularly grilled tucunaré and pirarucu.

activities.text: If your flight leaves in the evening, lunch at Praia da Graciosa with a peixe na telha (fish cooked on clay tile, local specialty) accompanied by farofa and rice. It's the Palmas version of the return-from-travel meal.

practical_info.tip: Avoid fish during the hottest hours, better digested early

activities.text: Stop by the Mercado Municipal for last purchases: pequi (cerrado fruit with powerful flavor), pamonha de Goiás, artisanal cachaça, and of course a few certified capim dourado pieces. Open early morning, quieter before 10 AM.

seo.h1_title: Jalapão: 7 Days in Tocantins' Water Desert
seo.intro_paragraph: In the heart of central Brazil, Jalapão concentrates 34,000 km² of preserved cerrado, orange dunes born from millennial erosion, springs where water makes you float, and unique quilombola craftsmanship. This 7-day 4x4 journey departs from Palmas, crosses Ponte Alta and Mateiros, and ends at Quilombo Mumbuca, where capim dourado has been woven since the early 20th century.
seo.seo_hook: Orange dunes, fervedouros that make you float, turquoise waterfalls and golden capim from a century-old quilombo - the raw adventure of the cerrado.
featured_list: Ponte Alta do Tocantins: park gateway and Sussuapara Canyon with its 25-meter red walls
Mateiros: Cachoeira da Velha (100 meters wide) and base for the Jalapão dunes
Cachoeira do Formiga: 8-meter emerald pool, natural hydromassage
Quilombo Mumbuca: capim dourado craftsmanship since 20th-century Xerente teachings

places_taglines.BR::ponte-alta-do-tocantins: Western gateway to Jalapão State Park.
places_taglines.BR::mateiros: Tourist capital of Jalapão, base for dunes and fervedouros.
places_taglines.BR::mumbuca: Afro-indigenous quilombo, birthplace of capim dourado craftsmanship.
places_taglines.BR::palmas: Planned capital of Tocantins, founded in 1989 on the Tocantins River.

highlights: Jalapão State Park covers 34,000 km² of preserved cerrado, one of the last great expanses of this Brazilian savanna
Fervedouros are artesian springs where water pressure makes you float effortlessly, a rare phenomenon worldwide
The Jalapão dunes formed through millennial erosion of Serra do Espírito Santo, the wind depositing sand in the same place
Capim dourado, a fine golden plant stem, is endemic to Jalapão and has been woven since the early 20th century by artisans of Quilombo Mumbuca

itineraries.title: Bahia Sertão - 5 Days Tracing Canudos, the Raso da Catarina, and the Rio São Francisco
itineraries.subtitle: Five days crossing the historic Bahian sertão, from Salvador to the ruins of Belo Monte, to the canyons of the São Francisco river.
itineraries.notes: Semi-arid climate, temperatures reaching 40 °C during the day. Dry season from May to October strongly recommended. Visiting the interior of the Estação Ecológica do Raso da Catarina is forbidden to the public outside scientific research—observation of Lear's macaws takes place at the Estação Biológica de Canudos (Biodiversitas) and in accessible buffer zones with local guide. Car required. Arrival airport: Salvador (SSA). Possible return airport: Paulo Afonso (PAV) or Aracaju (AJU).

map_keywords: Centro de Abastecimento Feira de Santana
Catedral Senhora Sant'Ana Feira de Santana
Museu Casa do Sertão Feira de Santana

visits.text: You start in Salvador, pick up your rental car, then take the BR-324 northwest to Feira de Santana, 116 km away. Count on 1h30 on dual carriageway. Feira de Santana is Bahia's second city and the historic crossroads of the tropeiros who drove cattle from the sertões to the capital—hence its name, 'Sant'Ana's Fair.' This stop saves you from tackling the Canudos route in one push (another 280 km tomorrow).

Drop your bags and head straight for the Centro de Abastecimento, the city's big covered market. You're standing in the commercial heart of the sertão: carne de sol, queijo coalho, rapadura, farinha, dried fruit, medicinal herbs. Spot the umbu and cajá stalls—typical fruits you'll encounter further along. It's the best sensory introduction to the sertão before you dive in tomorrow.

The Museu Casa do Sertão, on the UEFS campus, is worth a detour. You'll find the material culture of the sertanejo: saddles, bridles, cangaceiro clothing, ex-votos, cordel instruments. Valuable context before Canudos. Open weekdays, closed weekends—check hours before you leave.

activities.text: Dine at a local carniceria or carne de sol restaurant. The specialty here is sun-dried meat, grilled and served with aipim (cassava) and farofa. It's uncompromising, and it's exactly what you need to taste as you arrive in the sertão.

practical_info.tip: Ask for carne de sol com aipim and baião de dois on the side

activities.text: Before heading to the hotel, spend 30 minutes filling the tank, checking the tyres, and buying water in quantity. Starting tomorrow, you enter the semi-arid zone: petrol stations grow scarcer and temperatures rise fast.

practical_info.tip: At least 4-5 litres of water per person per day in the car

map_keywords: Capela Senhor dos Passos Monte Santo

visits.text: You leave Canudos on the BR-116 heading south, then branch off toward Monte Santo via Euclides da Cunha (formerly Cumbe, renamed after the author of 'Os Sertões'). Count on about 2h15 of driving. Monte Santo served as the main military base for the fourth expedition against Belo Monte in 1897—from here the troops marched on foot to Canudos, roughly 150 km away.

The Via Sacra de Monte Santo climbs 1,969 metres along the Serra do Piquaraçá. Twenty-five chapels mark the Stations of the Cross, created in the late 18th century by the Italian Capuchin friar Apolônio de Todi—one of Brazil's principal 'sacromontes,' listed by IPHAN in 1983. Antônio Conselheiro passed through around 1892 with his thousands of followers and repaired part of the steps—five years before these same places became the base of the army that came to fight him. Steep ascent, allow 1h30 to 2h return.

At the summit, the Santuário da Santa Cruz dominates the sertanejo plain. The main chapel preserves the images of Our Lady of Solitude, the Dead Christ, and Saint John the Evangelist. The 360° view sweeps across cerrado and caatinga: this is the landscape Antônio Conselheiro saw from the lookout, and it's in these expanses that he preached. The geographical effect hits you: you understand why the sertão was for him a land of election.

Back down in town, the Museu de Monte Santo occupies the former house of Coronel Galdino Andrade, from the early 20th century. Small collection but well curated on the tradition of romarias, Conselheiro's passage, and the city's history. Allow 45 minutes.

activities.text: If you're here on a Sunday or during Semana Santa, you'll encounter romeiros climbing to the sanctuary barefoot, sometimes on their knees, with stones on their heads. This tradition isn't folkloric—it's alive, and the All Saints' feasts (31 October - 1 November) draw thousands of pilgrims. Stay discreet, photograph respectfully.

Taste sertaneja cuisine in a restaurant on Rua Senhor dos Passos: bode guisado (kid stew), buchada de bode (stuffed tripe), galinha caipira, accompanied by a suco de umbu. These dishes are the staples of local cuisine, adapted to drought and the dominant goat farming.

practical_info.tip: Buchada de bode isn't for sensitive stomachs—ask what's in it first

visits.text: Long drive this morning: 281 km from Feira de Santana to Canudos via BR-116 then BR-235, count on about 4h of driving. The landscape gradually shifts, vegetation grows scrubby, mandacarus appear, distances stretch. You're entering the deep sertão. Make a coffee stop at Tucano or Euclides da Cunha to breathe and break the journey.

You arrive in Canudos early afternoon. The present town is the third of its name—emancipated in 1985 under this name from the district of Cocorobó. The first Belo Monte founded by Antônio Conselheiro in 1893 was razed on 5 October 1897, the second founded around 1910 by survivors was drowned by the Cocorobó dam in the 1960s. During drought, the ruins of the first Canudos emerge from the reservoir.

Head to the Parque Estadual de Canudos, created in 1986 and managed by the State of Bahia. You'll find the Alto do Mário, the Alto da Favela, and the Fazenda Velha—the sites of the fourth military expedition's battles led by General Artur Oscar with roughly 10,000 men. This is where the war ended on 5 October 1897, after twelve days of intense bombardment with Krupp cannon and the 105mm Whitworth 'matadeira.'

The Instituto Popular Memorial de Canudos (IPMC), separate from the state park, preserves Antônio Conselheiro's Cruzeiro riddled with bullets during the war, a collection of popular art inspired by Belo Monte, and a dedicated library. It's the other major historical stop in town—more intimate than the park, and complementary.

activities.text: If it's the dry season and the reservoir level is low, go down to the edge of the Cocorobó dam. You can see the ruins of the first Canudos emerge from the water—foundations, wall sections, traces of Belo Monte's urban layout. Striking effect when you have Euclides da Cunha's 'Os Sertões' (1902) in mind.

practical_info.tip: Ask locals about the reservoir level before going

activities.text: In the evening, settle into a local lanchonete and strike up conversation with the inhabitants. The memory of Canudos isn't just textbook material here—it's family history for several lineages. Many have ancestors among the survivors, and the event remains a strong identity reference for the Bahian sertão.

map_keywords: Cânion Rio São Francisco Paulo Afonso

visits.text: Long drive from Monte Santo to Paulo Afonso, roughly 280 km via BR-116 then BR-110, count on 4h30 with stops. To break the journey, stop at Jeremoabo, about 100 km from Paulo Afonso: it's one of the municipalities from where Lear's macaws are observed and the seat of the 'Programa de Conservação da Arara-Azul-de-Lear' created in 2001.

If your schedule allows, the Estação Biológica de Canudos, managed by the Fundação Biodiversitas (130 hectares acquired in 1993, expanded to 1,500 hectares in 2007), is one of the rare sites where visitors can observe Lear's macaws nesting in the sandstone cliffs. The species, endemic to the caatinga of northeastern Bahia, was rediscovered in 1978 by ornithologist Helmut Sick—it had been considered lost since its description by Bonaparte in 1856. Wild population estimated at around 2,000 individuals. Booking required in advance.

The Raso da Catarina protected area itself, instituted as an ecological reserve by decree 89.268 of 3 January 1984 then reclassified as Estação Ecológica in 2001, covers roughly 105,000 hectares of shrubby caatinga between the rios São Francisco and Vaza-Barris. Entry is forbidden to the public outside scientific research. What you can do: drive along the BR-110 between Paulo Afonso and Jeremoabo, observe the landscapes from the buffer zone, and—with a local guide—access certain viewpoints over the sandstone cliffs, refuge of cangaceiros in the early 20th century, including Lampião.

You arrive in Paulo Afonso late afternoon. The town owes its name to Paulo Viveiros Afonso, sertanista who received a sesmaria in 1725 including the cachoeira. Before 1948, it was a dusty village—the creation of CHESF (Companhia Hidrelétrica do São Francisco) that year transformed everything. The historic cachoeira, which impressed Dom Pedro II in 1859, is now dry because of the dams, except during exceptional floods.

activities.text: Dine facing the Rio São Francisco. The cuisine of the 'Velho Chico' (Old Frankie) revolves around freshwater fish: surubim, pintado, dourado. Pintado na telha, cooked on tile in its juice, is a classic. Also taste umbu juice, king fruit of the sertão, and local cachaça.

practical_info.tip: Several riverside restaurants serve fresh fish of the day

activities.text: Walk across the Ponte Metálica Dom Pedro II, 1950s ironwork on the BR-110 that links Paulo Afonso (Bahia) to Delmiro Gouveia (Alagoas). It marks the start of the São Francisco canyon and offers a plunging view over the first granite cliffs, up to roughly 50 metres above the water. It's also kilometre zero of the Xingó reservoir.

map_keywords: Cânion de Xingó Canindé do São Francisco
Museu de Arqueologia de Xingó MAX

visits.text: You cross the Ponte Metálica into Alagoas, then follow the south bank to Canindé do São Francisco (Sergipe), about 65 km from Paulo Afonso. This is where the catamarans embark for the Xingó canyon. The Xingó dam, closed on 10 June 1994, drowned the river's old corredeiras and created a 65 km reservoir, navigable to Paulo Afonso. Depth up to 180 metres between the granite cliffs.

Board for the canyon cruise. Count on roughly 2h30 of navigation through what's billed as 'the world's 5th navigable canyon'—local marketing formula, but the scenery delivers: emerald waters, orange walls, sparse caatinga atop the cliffs. Chance to swim in certain coves, and close passage of the caves known as Furnas do Morcego, which sheltered Lampião and his bando in the 1930s.

In Canindé, don't miss the Museu de Arqueologia de Xingó (MAX), managed by the Universidade Federal de Sergipe. Created in 2000, it preserves the archaeological material saved before the dam's flooding: ceramics, burials, lithic tools of prehistoric populations of the São Francisco valley. Scientific perspective on everything you've just seen disappear beneath the waters.

activities.text: To finish the trip, cross over to Piranhas in Alagoas, small listed colonial port on the opposite bank, historic base of Lampião's bando. The town preserves its 19th-century centre and offers 'Rota do Cangaço' tours that retrace the famous cangaceiro's end at Angicos in 1938. Count 1h30 on site before heading back to Paulo Afonso for the night, or pushing on to Aracaju (200 km) if your flight leaves from there.

practical_info.tip: Aracaju (AJU) 200 km away, return Paulo Afonso (PAV) 70 km—choose according to your return flight

activities.text: Final regional lunch at the port of Canindé: moqueca de surubim, sopa de pirarucu, or grilled pintado. The riverside restaurants serve fish caught that same morning. Close the loop with a coffee and a rapadura from the region.

seo.h1_title: Bahia Sertão - 5 Days Tracing Canudos, the Raso da Catarina, and the Rio São Francisco
seo.intro_paragraph: Five days in the deep sertão of Bahia, tracing the War of Canudos (1896-1897) immortalised by Euclides da Cunha in 'Os Sertões.' The route leads from Salvador to Feira de Santana, Canudos, Monte Santo, and Paulo Afonso, to the canyons of the Rio São Francisco near the Xingó dam. Preserved caatinga, historic sites, sertaneja cuisine, and the river at the heart of the journey.
seo.seo_hook: Five days, the historic sertão by car, the ruins of Belo Monte, Monte Santo's Via Sacra, Lear's macaws, and the São Francisco canyons.
featured_list: Feira de Santana: crossroads of the tropeiros, central market and sertão museum to set the scene
Canudos: State Park created in 1986, ruins of the first Belo Monte beneath Cocorobó reservoir
Monte Santo: Bahian sanctuary listed by IPHAN, Via Sacra of 1,969 m and 25 chapels
Paulo Afonso and Xingó: 65 km navigable canyon, cliffs up to 100 m, Lampião's refuge

places_taglines.BR::feira-de-santana: Where the sertão's cattle trails met before the long march to the capital
places_taglines.BR::canudos: Where Antônio Conselheiro's dream drowned in cannon fire and silence
places_taglines.BR::monte-santo: Stone steps climbing to heaven, where pilgrims and soldiers once crossed paths
places_taglines.BR::paulo-afonso: Where the Old Frankie cuts through granite and cangaceiros once hid in caves

highlights: Canudos State Park, created in 1986, preserving the sites of the 1896-1897 messianic war
Monte Santo, sacred Bahian sanctuary listed by IPHAN in 1983, military base of the fourth expedition against Canudos
Estação Ecológica do Raso da Catarina, roughly 105,000 hectares of preserved caatinga and primary habitat of Lear's macaw
São Francisco Canyon, 65 km of granite wall up to 50 m high between Paulo Afonso and the Xingó dam

itineraries.title: Chapada das Mesas - 6 Days of Canyons and Waterfalls in Southern Maranhão
itineraries.subtitle: You explore six days of red sandstone mesetas, turquoise pools, and the national park's most voluminous waterfalls, based from Carolina.
itineraries.notes: Dry season strongly recommended (May to October). Several waterfalls inside the National Park require a 4x4 vehicle and accredited guide. Nearest airport is Imperatriz (MA), about 220 km from Carolina. Some attractions are on private property and each entrance has a separate fee.

map_keywords: Igreja Matriz São Pedro de Alcântara Carolina

visits.text: You arrive in Carolina, your base camp for the next six days. The small town perched on the banks of the Tocantins River, at the border with Tocantins State, serves as the gateway to the entire chapada. Drop your bags, take a stroll around Praça da Matriz, and pass by the Igreja Matriz São Pedro de Alcântara. Spend the afternoon recovering, scouting local agencies (you'll need them for 4x4 access), and scheduling your passeios for the coming days. Book your National Park outings as soon as you arrive: group slots fill up fast during high season.

The Tocantins riverfront is where locals gather in late afternoon. The river runs wide and calm, marking the natural border with Tocantins State. Walk along the bank, sit on a bench, and watch the sun sink behind the distant mesetas rising on the horizon. A perfect teaser before the more spectacular landscapes awaiting over the next days.

Carolina's municipal market is the place to taste southern Maranhão cuisine. Bacuri, cupuaçu, cajá, buriti—the list of cerrado fruits runs long, and most will be unfamiliar. Also look for surubim no leite de coco and galinha caipira, two local specialties. Stock up on water and snacks for the excursions ahead.

activities.text: Planning your itinerary with a local agency is virtually mandatory here. National Park attractions (São Romão, Prata) are only accessible by 4x4 with an accredited guide, and roads aren't always signposted. Allow 2 to 3 hours to compare offers, confirm prices, and set your dates.

practical_info.tip: Bring cash—banking services are limited in the chapada

activities.text: If you arrive in early afternoon, take time for a meal at a typical restaurant to discover sertaneja cuisine: carne de sol with macaxeira (cassava), fried tambaqui fish, rice and beans. A solid introduction before the athletic days ahead.

practical_info.tip: Restaurant Queiroz (Rio Lajes) is a local favorite

map_keywords: Portal da Chapada das Mesas Carolina

visits.text: You start the day at the Pedra Caída complex, 36 km from Carolina on the BR-230 (Transamazonian Highway). Spread across 12,000 hectares with six visitable waterfalls, a cable car, and complete infrastructure—restaurant, changing rooms, pools. It's the most comfortable introduction to the chapada, and the most touristy. The complex entrance has a fee, then each waterfall and activity requires separate payment. Arrive at opening to hit the falls before the crowds.

Cachoeira do Santuário is what you came to see: 46 meters plunging into a red sandstone canyon. You reach it via a wooden walkway of about 600 meters, then a section wading through water between towering walls. The roar, the moist air, the ray of light striking the falls mid-morning—the effect is immediate. Guide mandatory.

Within the same complex, Cachoeira da Caverna offers 12 meters of falls accessed through a sandstone cave. You enter from behind, through the water curtain, making it more playful than spectacular. The pool reaches 5 meters deep, with safety ropes installed. The 400-meter trail is on wooden deck.

You complete the classic Pedra Caída triangle with Cachoeira do Capelão. A gentler fall than its Santuário neighbor, set amid ochre-red cliffs. The name comes from the capelão monkeys that once populated the canopy above the falls. Ideal for catching your breath between more demanding trails.

On the way back to Carolina, don't miss the Portal da Chapada (also called Pedra Furada), 19 km from town. The window carved into the sandstone cliff rises about 76 meters and is reached after 600 meters walking through sand. Several viewpoints along the trail offer views of Morro do Chapéu, the Pilares da Chapada, and Serra da Cangalha. Aim for sunset—the low-angle light sets the rock ablaze.

activities.text: If you're craving adrenaline, the Pedra Caída complex offers a zipline 1,400 meters long with about 300 meters of vertical drop, reaching nearly 80 km/h. You ride a cable car up—about 400 meters of cable climbing to the top of the chapada. Separate fee from the complex entrance.

practical_info.booking: Reserve at the complex reception desk

activities.text: Take advantage of the complex's pools mid-afternoon to recover between waterfalls. The complex entrance includes access to pools, changing rooms, and rest areas—useful when the mercury tops 35°C.

visits.text: Pre-dawn wake-up: you climb Morro do Chapéu at sunrise, otherwise the heat becomes unbearable. The trail starts about 13 km from Carolina, after 5 km on BR-230 then 8 km of sandy dirt road. Expect about an hour of steep ascent over soft sand then loose rock, with assistance ropes on technical sections. The summit is a broad plateau at 378 meters elevation—highest point in the chapada—from which you take in Carolina, the Tocantins River, Portal da Chapada, and Serra da Valença. Guide mandatory (the morro is in the national park buffer zone). Avoid rainy days: the sandstone attracts lightning.

In the afternoon, you head to Cachoeiras Gêmeas do Itapecuru, about 30 km from Carolina. Two parallel falls drop side by side into a swimming hole. The site has a restaurant overlooking the waterfalls and offers kayak rentals for paddling in the natural pool. A relaxed end to the day after the morning's exertion.

activities.text: Rent a kayak on the Cachoeiras Gêmeas pool to approach the twin falls from the water. The perspective shifts completely and you get a better sense of the wall heights. Allow 30 minutes to circle around at a leisurely pace.

Back in Carolina in late afternoon, head to the riverfront for sunset over the Tocantins. The red cliffs on the opposite bank catch fire. Many Carolinenses gather on the benches by the river at this hour—very local vibe.

map_keywords: Cachoeira de São Romão Chapada das Mesas National Park
Cachoeira do Prata Chapada das Mesas

visits.text: Today you truly enter Parque Nacional da Chapada das Mesas—159,953 hectares created in December 2005, at the meeting point of three biomes: Cerrado, Caatinga, and Amazonia. Very early departure from Carolina by 4x4 with accredited guide—it's the only option: sandy tracks are impassable without four-wheel drive. Expect about 30 km of pavement then 50 km of sandy dirt road, sometimes in poor condition, crossing private properties.

First stop on the route: Morro das Figuras, rock formations with strange silhouettes. According to ICMBio, human occupation of the chapada dates back over 10,000 years. The rock inscriptions on the small morro are sometimes attributed locally to Krahô populations (Jê linguistic family), though the attribution remains debated. Take a few photos before resuming the track—still a good two hours to São Romão.

Cachoeira de São Romão is the park's most voluminous waterfall, 25 meters high on the Farinha River (Tocantins tributary). You first arrive at the overlook from above, which gives the full measure, then descend to the natural pool at the base. A cave inhabited by swallows opens behind the water curtain—access possible depending on flow. The site includes a small restaurant, changing rooms, and kayak rental. Dry season (May-October) is by far the best; the river can become dangerous during the rains.

Sixteen kilometers from São Romão, you continue to Cachoeira do Prata. Two parallel falls of about 22 meters, which merge into a single water curtain during rainy season. A short trail with a small suspension footbridge leads to the river. The site is more for contemplation than swimming—the flow and rocks make swimming complicated.

activities.text: Rent a kayak on the São Romão natural pool, about 30 minutes of paddling. It's the easiest way to approach the water curtain and measure the force of the falls.

Lunch at one of the park's small restaurants, usually next to the cachoeira reception. Carne de sol cooked over wood fire, rice, tropeiro beans, cassava—sertaneja cuisine served in a plank kitchen. Reservations recommended as facilities are rudimentary.

practical_info.booking: The guide normally handles the reservation

visits.text: You leave Carolina for Riachão, about 100 km southeast, the chapada's second gateway. The road alternates between pavement and 25 to 30 km of dirt depending on the sites you're targeting. A gentler day after yesterday's 4x4 marathon. You overnight in Riachão to save time on the northeastern chapada attractions.

You discover Encanto Azul in early afternoon, a small lake formed by a spring gushing between rock walls. The color—saturated turquoise blue—comes from the white sand bottom and light filtering through the vegetation. No infrastructure on site except restrooms; entrance is very cheap. Arrive early—by 9 a.m. the site fills up and the perfect photo requires perfectly still water.

Six kilometers from Encanto Azul, you continue to Poço Azul, part of Reserva Natural Cachoeira do Rio Cocal. A private property crossed by the Cocal River, which forms four waterfalls in total. The poço itself is a deep blue pool where you can swim, depending on river conditions. Good infrastructure: buffet restaurant, changing rooms, rest area. During heavy rains, swimming may be prohibited for safety reasons.

activities.text: If you're ready to push on to Cachoeira Santa Bárbara, expect an additional walk on a suspension footbridge of about 700 meters. The falls stand 76 meters high, the chapada's tallest. Depending on season and conditions, swimming at the base may or may not be possible—call ahead to check.

practical_info.tip: Let them know when you arrive at Poço Azul—it's in the same complex

activities.text: For thrill-seekers, Poço Azul offers rappelling down Cachoeira Santa Bárbara—70 meters to the natural pool. Supervised activity, book in the morning on site subject to availability.

practical_info.booking: Reserve on site, depends on weather and flow

visits.text: You leave Riachão early morning to return to Carolina, about 100 km of road. The route passes back through the meseta landscapes you're starting to recognize. Make a stop on BR-230 to photograph Morro do Chapéu from a distance—its upturned-hat silhouette is recognizable from kilometers away and explains the name. Arrival in Carolina around noon, drop bags at the hotel, and lunch in town before the afternoon's passeio.

In the afternoon, head to the Tocantins River for the boat passeio to Pedra Encantada. The excursion lasts about 4 hours, departing around 3 p.m. You travel upriver 25 km through an area held back by the Estreito hydroelectric dam—calm, warm waters. Pedra Encantada is an 8-meter rock formation in the middle of the river, which the brave climb to jump into the water. The outing ends with sunset over the Tocantins, with the mesetas in silhouette.

To close out the trip, cross the Tocantins River by ferry (balsa) to Filadélfia in neighboring Tocantins State. The small river beach is where Carolina residents spend their weekends. Light sand, warm water, a few rustic bars—a quiet end to the journey before heading home.

activities.text: On the river passeio, jump from Pedra Encantada into the Tocantins if the urge strikes—8 meters above the water, the jump is clean and the pool deep enough. Optional activity; the boat stops at the base of the rock. Not required to enjoy the outing.

In the evening, final dinner in Carolina at a typical restaurant: fried tambaqui, surubim in coconut milk, or galinha caipira. Accompany with bacuri or cupuaçu juice, fruits you won't easily find elsewhere.

practical_info.tip: Try bacuri, nicknamed 'cerrado gold'

seo.h1_title: Chapada das Mesas - 6 Days of Canyons and Waterfalls in Southern Maranhão
seo.intro_paragraph: Six days in Chapada das Mesas, southern Maranhão, from the town of Carolina. You hit the Pedra Caída waterfalls, the summit of Morro do Chapéu, the monumental São Romão and Prata falls in the National Park, then the turquoise waters of Poço Azul and Encanto Azul near Riachão. An itinerary designed for lovers of nature, swimming, and raw landscapes.
seo.seo_hook: Six days, Carolina as base camp, the National Park by 4x4, Morro do Chapéu at sunrise, the Tocantins by boat.
featured_list: Carolina: base town on the Tocantins River, access point to all southern Maranhão attractions
Pedra Caída: Cachoeira do Santuário plunging 46 meters into a sandstone canyon, plus 1,400-meter zipline
National Park: São Romão (25 m, most voluminous) and Prata, accessible only by 4x4 with guide
Riachão: Poço Azul, Encanto Azul, and Cachoeira Santa Bárbara (76 m, tallest in the chapada)

places_taglines.BR::carolina: Gateway town on the Tocantins River, where the chapada begins
places_taglines.BR::riachao: Where turquoise springs emerge from the cerrado bedrock

highlights: Cachoeira do Santuário, 46-meter waterfall plunging into a sandstone canyon at Pedra Caída
Cachoeira de São Romão, 25 meters high and the most voluminous in the National Park
Morro do Chapéu, sandstone plateau at 378 meters elevation with 360° views over the cerrado
Parque Nacional da Chapada das Mesas, 159,953 hectares created in 2005, at the meeting point of Cerrado, Caatinga, and Amazonia

itineraries.title: Chapada Diamantina - Brazil's Wild Diamond Mining Heartland
itineraries.subtitle: Eight days in the Northeast's most spectacular national park, trekking through waterfalls, quartz caves, tabletop plateaus and a three-day Vale do Pati expedition.
itineraries.notes: Arrival and departure via Salvador (international flights via GRU or direct from Europe). Salvador → Lençóis: 415 km, 6h via BR-324 then BR-242, significantly faster by Azul regional flight (1h, 3 weekly rotations). All excursions depart from Lençóis or Vale do Capão. Pack good hiking boots, rain poncho, swimsuit and waterproof bag. Climate: dry season May to September, ideal for trekking. Poços Azul and Encantado: blue light effect only September to March (midday sun striking the cave).

visits.text: Arrive in Salvador de Bahia via Luís Eduardo Magalhães International Airport. Transfer to Pelourinho, 30 km, 45 min. Drop your bags at a pousada in the historic center. The afternoon is enough for your first immersion into Brazil's Afro-Brazilian capital.

Pelourinho, a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1985, was the central square of Brazil's first capital (1549-1763) from the 16th to 18th centuries. Its name recalls the whipping post where slaves were punished. The restored pastel facades, Igreja Nossa Senhora do Rosário dos Pretos and Praça Terreiro de Jesus form South America's most cohesive colonial ensemble.

The Igreja e Convento de São Francisco ranks among the world's baroque masterpieces. Austere exterior facade, interior entirely covered with 800 kilos of gold leaf on carved wood, Portuguese azulejo tiles lining the cloister walls. Built between 1708 and 1723 by Franciscan friars. Admission 10 BRL.

Take the Elevador Lacerda, an Art Deco elevator from 1873 that connects the upper city to the lower city in 30 seconds (72 m drop). Obligatory postcard shot. Fare 1 BRL since the February 2025 reopening (free with SalvadorCard). Once down, head to Mercado Modelo for handicrafts (capoeira, berimbaus, hammocks) and views over Baía de Todos os Santos.

activities.text: Dine in Pelourinho at a Bahian restaurant. Signature dishes: moqueca de peixe (fish in coconut milk and dendê oil), acarajé (black-eyed pea fritters fried in palm oil), vatapá. Uauá or Axego on Rua João de Deus are local favorites that avoid the tourist traps.

practical_info.tip: Reservations recommended on weekends

activities.text: On Tuesday evenings, catch free capoeira and Afro-percussion shows on Praça Pedro Archanjo. Groups like Olodum and Filhos de Gandhy perform regularly. Unique atmosphere, but keep an eye on your belongings. Avoid isolated alleys at night.

to_next_leg.road_type: Azul flight Salvador-Lençóis (1h) or 6h bus via BR-324/BR-242

visits.text: Depart Salvador → Lençóis. The Azul flight (3 weekly rotations, 1h) is highly recommended: 6h by bus via BR-242 in comparison. Horácio de Matos Airport, 20 km from downtown Lençóis, 30 min transfer.

Lençóis, founded in 1845, was the capital of Brazil's diamond boom. Its name comes from the white tents of garimpeiros seen from the hilltops, which resembled sheets (lençóis). Population 10,000, but the town was Bahia's third-largest at its peak. It has preserved its colonial street plan and multicolored architectural heritage.

Stroll along Rua das Pedras and Praça Otaviano Alves. Low pastel-facade houses, artisan workshops, former diamond merchants' mansions. The old French vice-consulate (1873), now the Subprefeitura, recalls when the town dealt directly with European buyers. Stop by the Museu do Garimpo for context.

Afternoon at Rio Serrano, accessible on foot from the center (20 min). Natural pools carved into red sandstone, polished stone slides, 2-3 m waterfalls. The water takes on a characteristic amber tint from plant tannins. Perfect swimming spot to recover from the journey.

Continue upstream to Ribeirão do Meio, famous for its 10-meter natural waterslide. The rock polished by centuries of erosion allows a cushioned slide into a large pool. Watch out for collisions with other swimmers on busy days. Municipal park entrance 5 BRL.

activities.text: Dine on Rua das Pedras, the pedestrian shopping street in the center. Neco's serves a reimagined moqueca chapadeira (freshwater fish), Lampião offers a traditional Bahian menu. Count on 60-90 BRL per person with drinks.

Book your Vale do Pati trek for D5-D7 with a trusted agency: Chapada Adventure, Raízes da Chapada, Lençóis Trekking, Extrema Expedições. A briefing the day before is typically scheduled. Count on 1800-2500 BRL for 3 days all-inclusive (guide, family homestay accommodation, meals).

practical_info.booking: Book at least 1 week in advance

visits.text: Big day as a classic "caves and Pai Inácio tour," the essential must-do excursion. Depart 8:30 AM from Lençóis by van with an agency. Route of 150 km total by car, 6 km of walking spread throughout the day. Return around 6:30 PM. About 200 BRL all-inclusive with lunch.

First stop at Cachoeira do Mosquito, 40 min from Lençóis. Short walk (20 min one-way) to a 25 m waterfall framed by ochre rocks. Swimming in the natural pool below. Then 800 m rocky trail to Poço do Diabo, another waterfall in a more dramatic canyon. Deep natural pool.

Gruta da Lapa Doce, 80 km west, is Brazil's third-largest cave (42 km mapped, 850 m visitable). The main chamber rises 70 m high. Stalactites, stalagmites, columns, calcite curtains, cathedral formations. Visit with guide and mandatory headlamp (45 min).

Fazenda Pratinha, a neighboring private property, features Gruta Azul (blue light reflecting the crystal-clear water at 2:30 PM in dry season) and Gruta da Pratinha, where a snorkeling float (40 BRL supplement) lets you explore 150 m underground. Transparent waters populated by small fish and bat nests on the ceiling.

Finish at Morro do Pai Inácio. 20 min ascent, 250 m elevation gain on rocky trail. The tabletop plateau at 1120 m altitude offers 360° panorama over Serra do Sincorá, Morro do Camelo and Morrão. The low-angle sunset light sets the orange cliffs ablaze. Absolute Chapada postcard.

activities.text: Lunch included in the tour, at Fazenda Pratinha. Simple but generous Mineiro-Bahian buffet (55-70 BRL). Wood-fired chicken, rice and beans, fresh salads. Good mid-route break.

Return to Lençóis around 6:30 PM. Dinner at a downtown restaurant: Cozinha Aberta (chef who worked in São Paulo, revisited chapadeira cuisine) or the classic Picanha na Praça for grilled meat. Quiet evening, prep gear for D4.

visits.text: Second major excursion: Poço Azul, Poço Encantado and Igatu village. Full day, 286 km of road, little walking but lots of driving. Depart 8 AM, return 5 PM. About 250 BRL with lunch and admission fees.

Poço Encantado, 150 km from Lençóis in the municipality of Itaetê, is a limestone cavity sheltering an underground lake 61 m deep. From September to March, between 10:30 AM and 1 PM, sunlight passes through a natural crack and illuminates the water in fluorescent cobalt blue. Swimming strictly forbidden to preserve the site.

Poço Azul, 20 km from Poço Encantado, shares the same light phenomenon with one major advantage: swimming is allowed. Floating with snorkel mask, mandatory vest, in 22-24°C water of absolute transparency. The golden noon light transforms the experience into a golden underwater ballet.

On the return, detour to Igatu, nicknamed "Brazil's Machu Picchu." Dry-stone village built in the 19th century by garimpeiros. Ruins of houses right in the village, picturesque cemetery, Museu do Garimpo installed in a former miner's house. Current population: 350 inhabitants.

In Igatu itself, the Ruínas do Garimpo, 15 min walk from the center, are the entirely abandoned former mining quarter. Dry-stone walls erected without mortar, remains of sorting workshops, cactus-lined paths. Desert-like and silent atmosphere, feels like a Western movie set.

activities.text: Lunch included at Igatu in one of the simple downtown restaurants. Flor de Açucena serves typical family cooking: feijoada, galinha caipira, homemade sweets (guava, banana, umbu preserves). Stone setting, warm atmosphere.

Return to Lençóis in the evening. Last evening before the trek: light dinner, early to bed, pack backpack for 3 days. The agency usually provides the list: technical clothing, rain poncho, headlamp, 2-3 liters of water, sunglasses, sunscreen, insect repellent.

practical_info.tip: Backpack 35-45 L max, all surplus stays at hotel

to_next_leg.road_type: dirt road at end of route

visits.text: First day of Vale do Pati trek. Wake 5 AM, 1h30 transfer to the village of Guiné (Palmeiras municipality), main starting point. Briefing with guide: safety, route, eco-responsibility. No cell phone signal for the next 3 days.

Vale do Pati is regularly ranked among the world's 10 best treks. This isolated valley, formed by the Rio Pati between Palmeiras, Andaraí and Mucugê, is accessible only on foot. Eleven families still live here today (400 at the garimpeiro peak), welcoming trekkers in their family guesthouses since 1997.

Trail starts via Aleixos: 1h sustained climb (300 m elevation gain). At the summit, brutal panoramic opening onto the valley. Crossing the Gerais do Rio Preto, high-altitude plateau covered with candombás (endemic plants), small streams forded. Rocky terrain, well-marked trail.

Descent toward the Mirante do Vale do Pati. Plunging view over the valley framed by tabletop serras. The villages (Igrejinha, Campo das Flores, Ruinha) appear as white dots in the forest. You understand why the site is called a "hidden green utopia" by trekkers.

Final descent of 1h30, 500 m negative elevation, steep and rocky trail, toward the hamlet of Igrejinha. Arrival in late afternoon at Seu Wilson and Maria's, pioneers of valley hospitality since 1997. Simple room, decent bed, cold shower, solar electricity 3 hours in the evening.

activities.text: Dinner as a family at Seu Wilson's. Rice, black beans, dried beef (carne-de-sol), vegetables from the garden, doces de leite for dessert. Wood-fired cooking. Eat at a communal table with other trekkers. Chatting, singing, guitar. Unique atmosphere.

Early to bed (9 PM), starry night without light pollution. Milky Way visible to the naked eye. Temperatures can drop below 15°C in June-July: wool and down recommended. Natural wake-up to roosters and birds at 6 AM.

visits.text: Second trek day, the most spectacular. Depart 7 AM with picnic prepared by Maria. Head for Morro do Castelo, a 1300 m rocky peak visible from across the valley. Traverse a short cave by headlamp before the final climb.

Morro do Castelo peaks at 1300 m. 360° panorama over the tabletop serras, the caatinga below, sometimes even Cachoeira da Fumaça in the distance on clear days. One of the park's best observation points. Summit picnic: pão, queijo, rapadura, fruit.

Descent toward Cachoeirão, Brazil's second-highest waterfall (280 m). Observation from the top of the canyon, 260 m sheer cliff. In the rainy season, over 20 distinct water streams fall into the mist. The morning rainbows are legendary. No swimming possible at this spot.

Return via alternate trail through Cachoeira dos Funis and Poço da Árvore. 50 m waterfall in three tiers, deep pool where swimming is allowed. Water at 18°C, bracing but regenerating after the effort. Lunch at the pool's edge.

Return to Seu Wilson's in late afternoon, 1h30 walk. Cold shower (or icy in June-July), siesta, reading. Some try the neighboring families' tabuleiro for fresh rapadura. The valley's total silence is one of the stay's most striking elements.

activities.text: Simple and hearty dinner: hot soup, galinha caipira (free-range chicken stew), rice, corn angu, cabbage salad. Many trekkers linger around the table: the absence of screens recreates forgotten conviviality. Local artisanal cachaça as digestif.

Free astronomical observation from the pousada courtyard. Zero light pollution, Milky Way crossing the sky. Some guides do impromptu star chart animations (Cruz do Sul, Escorpião, Centauro). Bedtime 9:30 PM.

visits.text: Last trek day, exit via Vale do Capão. Wake 5:30 AM, full breakfast (pão de queijo, eggs, coffee, fruit) then farewell to Seu Wilson. Long day: 6h walking, 700 m cumulative elevation, varied landscapes.

Sustained 2h climb toward Gerais do Vieira, high-altitude plateau (1200-1400 m). Low vegetation, candombás and endemic orchids. Former garimpeiro path, recognizable by stone cairns (mundéus) marking the route. Mandatory breathing break and hydration at the summit.

Descent toward Mirante da Fumaça, natural overlook at 1040 m altitude. From this viewpoint, direct view of Cachoeira da Fumaça dropping 340 to 380 m below into a canyon. In dry season, winds carry the pulverized water upward creating the "smoke" effect that gives the waterfall its name. Weightless sensation guaranteed.

Final descent toward Vale do Capão, 3h walk. Traverse humid Atlantic forest, crystalline streams, orchids and bromeliads. Arrival at the village of Caeté-Açu, called Vale do Capão, an alternative neo-hippie village founded in the 1970s by residents seeking counter-culture. 1500 inhabitants, unique vibe.

activities.text: Late lunch at Vale do Capão in a vegetarian restaurant (Restaurante Capão, Santo Grão). Large veggie and Hare Krishna community in the village: palm soup, vegetable lasagna, fresh tropical juices. Radical change after 3 days of rustic cooking. Count on 45-60 BRL.

4x4 transfer from Vale do Capão to Lençóis in late afternoon, 1h30 drive partly unpaved. Arrival in Lençóis around 7 PM. Extended shower, laundry, quiet evening. Light dinner downtown, early to bed. The muscle soreness will be there tomorrow morning.

practical_info.tip: Included in most trek packages

visits.text: Free morning in Lençóis. Options depending on your flight time: easy walk to Poço Halley (20 min from center, quiet swimming), visit the Museu do Garimpo we didn't have time for on D2, handicraft shopping (semi-precious stone jewelry, banana and umbu sweets).

More active option: half-day excursion to Marimbus, wetland nicknamed the Chapada's "mini-Pantanal." Canoe crossing on Rio Roncador amid buritis (marsh palms), water lilies, jacaré alligators and aquatic birds. Count on 150 BRL per person, 4h all-inclusive.

Transfer to Horácio de Matos Airport, 20 km, 30 min. Azul flight Lençóis-Salvador (1h) mid-afternoon. Three weekly frequencies: absolutely verify the day before booking. Alternative if no flight: Real Expresso bus Lençóis-Salvador (6h, 120 BRL).

Arrive in Salvador in the evening. Depending on international return flight time, extra night downtown or direct transfer to Luís Eduardo Magalhães Airport. Flights to Europe typically depart between 10 PM and 11 PM (via GRU or direct).

activities.text: If staying overnight in Salvador, final Bahian dinner in the chic Rio Vermelho neighborhood (Boteco do França, Paraíso Tropical). Traditional moqueca, authentic dendê, passion fruit caipirinha. Last moment of Bahian spirit before returning home.

Bring back artisanal Chapada cachaça: Seleta or Bocoy brands, produced in Vale do Paraguaçu. On sale at Lençóis Municipal Market or the airport. Count on 60-100 BRL per artisanal bottle. Checked baggage required for international flights.

practical_info.tip: Shock-resistant packaging + zip bags to secure in hold

seo.h1_title: Chapada Diamantina - Brazil's Wild Diamond Mining Heartland
seo.intro_paragraph: Eight days deep in the Northeast's most spectacular national park, 415 km from Salvador. Lençóis, the former diamond capital, its monumental waterfalls, limestone caves, the hilltop village of Igatu, turquoise-blue natural pools and above all the 3-day Vale do Pati trek, ranked among the world's best, with overnight stays in family homes. A concentrated dose of raw nature, trekking and mining history.
seo.seo_hook: Cachoeira da Fumaça 380 m, Morro do Pai Inácio, Poço Azul, 3-day Vale do Pati trek with family homestays, Lapa Doce caves and the stone village of Igatu in a loop from Salvador.
featured_list: Vale do Pati, trek ranked among the world's best: 3 days, family homestays, no cell phone signal
Cachoeira da Fumaça: 340-380 m free fall, Brazil's second-highest waterfall
Morro do Pai Inácio: tabletop plateau at 1120 m, 360° panorama at sunset
Poço Azul and Poço Encantado: underground lakes in fluorescent blue from September to March
Gruta da Lapa Doce: Brazil's third-largest cave, chambers rising 70 m high
Igatu: "Brazil's Machu Picchu," 19th-century garimpeiro stone village
Lençóis: historic diamond capital (1845), multicolored colonial center, national heritage site
Salvador and Pelourinho: Brazil's first colonial center (1549), UNESCO 1985

places_taglines.BR::salvador: Brazil's first capital, UNESCO 1985, Afro-Bahian heartland and baroque Pelourinho.
places_taglines.BR::lencois: Historic diamond capital (1845), main base for exploring the national park.
places_taglines.BR::vale-do-pati: Hidden valley ranked Brazil's best trek, 11 families still welcome hikers into their homes.
places_taglines.BR::vale-do-capao: Alternative village and gateway to Cachoeira da Fumaça, 380 m of free-falling water.

highlights: Vale do Pati, voted among the world's best treks: 3 days in the valleys, family homestays, no cell phone signal
Cachoeira da Fumaça, 340-380 m free fall, one of Brazil's highest waterfalls and the tallest easily accessible, named for the smoke effect of the spray
Morro do Pai Inácio, 1120 m, the park's postcard: tabletop plateau offering 360° panorama at sunset
Poço Azul and Poço Encantado, underground lakes with fluorescent turquoise-blue waters (light effect September-March)
Igatu, "Brazil's Machu Picchu": stone village from the 19th-century garimpeiro era
Lençóis, historic diamond capital (1845), colorful colonial center classified as Brazilian national heritage

itineraries.title: Fortaleza, Jericoacoara & Gateway to the Lençóis: 7 Days on Brazil's Northeast Coast
itineraries.subtitle: You follow 700 km of coastline between red cliffs, shifting dunes, and turquoise lagoons, from Fortaleza to the gateway of the Lençóis Maranhenses via the Parnaíba Delta.
itineraries.notes: Jericoacoara has no direct airport from Europe: you either enter from Fortaleza (4.5 hours drive + 4x4 transfer) or from the small airport in Jijoca 25 km away. The village streets are sand, no pavement, no public lighting—bring a headlamp. The 4x4 crossing between Jeri and Parnaíba only happens at low tide: impossible to improvise, book with a recognized operator (Jeri Off Road, Tropical Adventure). For the Lençóis, the best period is June to September when the lagoons are filled. From January to March, it's dry and far less spectacular.

visits.text: The Mercado Central in Fortaleza is a four-story cube planted right in the city center, just steps from the cathedral. You climb via helical ramps, and each level has its specialty: leather goods on the ground floor, colorful hammocks on the first (Ceará is Brazil's largest hammock producer), lace and embroidery on the second, cachaça and spices up top. It's touristy but vibrant, and prices are negotiable. Budget an hour, more if you fall for a hammock—bring a dollar per meter as a benchmark.

The Metropolitan Cathedral, right next to the Mercado, surprises with its sheer scale: 75 meters high, 5,000 seats, Brazil's third-largest church. Inaugurated in 1978 after more than forty years of construction, its neo-Gothic style with Romanesque touches contrasts sharply with the wooden stalls of the neighboring market. Modern stained glass in colored diamond patterns. Quick visit, but the juxtaposition is worth seeing.

The Centro Dragão do Mar de Arte e Cultura occupies a former port warehouse converted in 1999. You'll find the Ceará Museum, a cinematheque, and the Memorial da Cultura Cearense—this last one truly deserves the stop. It tells the story of sertão culture, the life of the cangaceiros (Northeast bandits like Lampião), drought, and forró music. The surrounding neighborhood, Praia de Iracema, becomes Fortaleza's nightlife hub after dark.

Praia do Futuro stretches for 8 km south of the city, lined with barracas—those massive thatched restaurant-pavilions that can accommodate hundreds of people. Chao do Mar, Crocobeach, Itapariká: each has its pool, stage, and vibe. Thursday nights feature the "caranguejada": all-you-can-eat crab while listening to live forró. The beach itself is windier and rawer than the upscale beaches, but it's the real seaside life of Fortaleza.

activities.text: Sample Cearense cuisine at a barraca on Praia do Futuro. The absolute specialty is peixada, a fish stew with coconut milk served with pirão (manioc flour puree) and rice. Baião de dois (rice with mung beans and fresh cheese) often accompanies dried meat. To drink: caldo de cana (sugarcane juice) or cajá caipirinha, made with the Northeast's tart fruit.

End the evening on Beira Mar, the long pedestrian avenue along the ocean. The night craft market, open every evening, lines up 600 stalls of hammocks, embroidery, sculptures, and leather goods. You can also grab a quick bite of stuffed tapioca or grilled churrasco. Perfect stroll for digesting.

visits.text: Canoa Quebrada clings to ochre-red cliffs that plunge into the Atlantic. The village lived off fishing until the 1970s, when French hippies heading to India ended up staying. On the cliffs, you'll still find the large crescent moon and star carved during that era, now the village symbol. The main street, nicknamed Broadway, is pedestrian-only and lined with bars, restaurants, and shops. Walk it at sunset when the cliffs turn copper.

Praia das Fontes, 30 km back toward Beberibe, owes its name to freshwater springs that gush directly through the cliff face. You climb a trail along the ridge then descend at low tide to see the water bubble up from the sandstone. The nearby Mãe d'Água cave was a refuge for escaped slaves in the 19th century. Perfect stop on the route between Fortaleza and Canoa.

Aracati, 13 km from Canoa, deserves a 30-minute detour for its listed colonial center. Facades with blue-and-white Portuguese azulejos, the 18th-century do Rosário church, narrow alleys leading to the Rio Jaguaribe. It's one of Ceará's rare examples of colonial architecture, and nobody stops—you'll likely have it to yourselves.

activities.text: Take the classic buggy tour along the cliffs and neighboring beaches. The driver (bugueiro) asks "com ou sem emoção?"—answer "com" if you want the vertiginous sand descents and dune stunts. Three hours round-trip to Lagoa do Coqueiro and Majorlândia beach. Swimming stops, zip-lining over the lagoon, grilled fish lunch. It's the iconic Ceará experience.

practical_info.booking: Book through your pousada, official bugueiros only

activities.text: If you arrive late afternoon, climb the cliffs on foot south of the village to watch the sunset. Praia do Amor stretches below, deserted. A few jangadas (fishing rafts with triangular sails) return to port, their silhouettes outlined against the horizon. This is Canoa's postcard image.

visits.text: Jericoacoara didn't get electricity until 1998, and the village deliberately keeps its rustic charm. No paved streets, no streetlights, no neon signs. The streets are deep sand—your shoes go in the bag as soon as you arrive. You can walk the entire village in 15 minutes, between Rua Principal and Rua do Forró. Wooden pousadas, passing donkeys, barefoot kids playing soccer. Drop your bags, stretch out, you've arrived.

The Duna do Pôr do Sol is Jeri's obligatory ritual. Every evening, about an hour before sunset, the entire village climbs this 30-meter dune just west of the last houses. You arrive breathless, the sand still warm underfoot, and wait. When the sun touches the Atlantic horizon, everyone applauds. It's cliché, it's touristy, it's also surprisingly moving. Bring a beer and stay after—the sky turns violet for a good 20 minutes.

At the opposite end of the village, 3 km east, Pedra Furada is the rock arch carved by the ocean that's become Jeri's symbol. You can reach it on foot only at low tide (check the tide tables posted everywhere in the village)—otherwise you get trapped in a cove. One-hour walk along the beach, with views of the Serrote cliffs along the way. Magical at sunrise when light passes through the opening.

activities.text: Try kitesurfing if you're here between July and December, the season of steady trade winds at 25 knots. Jeri is one of the three or four best spots in the world for the sport, and several schools offer beginner packages: 6 hours of lessons spread over 2-3 days, about 350 euros all-in. French-language instruction available. Thermal wind in the afternoon, perfect conditions.

practical_info.tip: Book before arrival in high season

activities.text: Dine at one of the village's discreet gastronomic spots. Tamarindo, in a candlelit interior courtyard, serves Mediterranean-Cearense cuisine at a surprising level for a village at the end of a dirt track. Sky Restaurant, on a pousada rooftop, makes cocktails with cajá and tamarind facing the sunset. Book ahead, options remain limited.

practical_info.booking: Reserve the day before in high season

visits.text: Lagoa do Paraíso (officially Lagoa Jijoca) is Jeri's other iconic image: translucent blue-green water, white sand, floating hammocks installed directly in the shallow water. You reach it by buggy or 4x4 (45 minutes from the village). Several barracas share the shores—Alchymist and Rancho do Peixe are the references. Reserve a hammock, order grilled fish and a local kiwi caipirinha, and you won't budge all day. Water at 28°C, no current, feet in the sand.

Lagoa Azul, smaller and less crowded, sits 5 km from Lagoa do Paraíso. Even clearer water, calmer atmosphere, ideal if you want to escape the groups. Often combined in the same buggy circuit. A few more modest barracas, gentle prices.

Mangue Seco, 12 km east of Jeri, is a mangrove tongue where the river meets the ocean. At low tide, you descend the waterway by stand-up paddle or kayak, framed by the aerial roots of mangroves. Red crabs, herons, sometimes a curassow. Excursions organized from the village, usually combined with a swimming stop at a nearby lagoon.

activities.text: Do the lagoon circuit by buggy: it's Jeri's classic full-day excursion. Departure around 9 AM, stops at Lagoa do Paraíso, Lagoa Azul, lunch on-site, return via the moving dunes at sunset with a stop at Pedra Furada if the tide permits. Budget 70 to 90 euros for a buggy with driver, split among up to 4 people. Ask for a bugueiro who speaks some English or French.

In the evening, head down to Praia do Forró for "capoeira na praia." Every evening around 8 PM, local capoeiristas set up a roda on the beach around a fire. Berimbau, atabaque drums, singing, and anyone can enter the circle. Not an organized show, no paid entry—it's just the village's nightlife.

visits.text: The Jeri-Parnaíba crossing by 4x4 is an adventure in itself: 6 hours of dirt tracks, beaches, and river crossings at low tide. You leave Jeri around 7 AM, first stop at Tatajuba (1 hour drive)—a village that was buried by moving dunes in 1990 and rebuilt 5 km away. The old church bell tower still emerges from the sand sometimes. Obligatory photo, swim in nearby Lagoa Torta.

You then cross several small ferries to ford the rivers between Tatajuba and Camocim, then follow deserted beaches to Barra Grande, a kitesurf station in Piauí. One-hour lunch stop, swimming. The track then continues through Piauí dunes to Parnaíba, your overnight stop. It's exhausting, bumpy, magnificent. Stay well hydrated, bring a scarf against the sand.

Parnaíba itself is a stopover town without great tourist appeal, but Porto dos Tatus, 12 km from the center, deserves an evening look. This is where the lanchas depart for the delta. Small wooden port on stilts, fish shacks, moored jangadas. You can already breathe the atmosphere of tomorrow's adventure.

activities.text: If you arrive early enough in Parnaíba, dine at Coco Bambu restaurant by the river: grilled tilapia, delta lobster, açaí for dessert. Get to bed early—the lancha for the delta leaves at 8 AM tomorrow and the night was short.

If the fatigue from the crossing still leaves you with energy, swim at sunset at Praia da Pedra do Sal, 15 km from Parnaíba. Fairly wild beach in Piauí state, red cliffs and interior lagoon. Few people even in high season.

visits.text: The Parnaíba Delta is one of only three open-ocean deltas in the world (along with the Nile and Mekong) and the only one in the Americas. 2,700 km², 75 islands, a labyrinth of waterways lined with mangroves, straddling Piauí and Maranhão. Departure by lancha (fast boat) at 8 AM from Porto dos Tatus, full day on the water. Stops to observe guarás (scarlet ibis) at sunset, swimming in an interior lagoon, grilled fish lunch on Morro do Meio island.

Ilha do Caju, in the Maranhão part of the delta, is a private 100 km² reserve belonging to the same family since 1847. You'll find about ten different ecosystems and a superb ecolodge for those who want to extend their stay. Group tours don't all stop here—specify if you want to see it, on a private tour. Manatees, armadillos, alligators in the interior rivers.

Before returning, operators often make a final stop on the sandbars that emerge at low tide right in the middle of the delta. Several hectares of immaculate white sand that will disappear under water in a few hours. The feeling of being on a floating beach, golden light on the return. Get your camera ready.

activities.text: Taste the delta's blue crab (caranguejo-uçá) on your return. Several barracas at Porto dos Tatus serve it still live, freshly shelled, with a sauce of coconut milk and cow's milk (the "caranguejo toada"). It's messy, you eat with your fingers, you emerge as happy diners. Count on a good hour.

For those with time before nightfall, go birdwatching in the mangroves at dusk by kayak. Several delta ecolodges organize this outing as a complement to the lancha tour. You glide silently between the aerial roots, and scarlet ibis arrive by the hundreds for the night. One of the trip's most memorable images.

visits.text: Barreirinhas is the main gateway to the Lençóis Maranhenses, a 5-hour drive from Parnaíba. Arrival mid-day, quick check-in at the pousada, then first foray into the park in the afternoon. The town lives entirely off park tourism: 4x4 agencies, restaurants, buriti craft shops. Lively main street, but forget the "authentic village" aspect—it's a logistical base.

The Lençóis Maranhenses is your trip's visual shock. 1,550 km² of white dunes stretching for 70 km, dotted with thousands of turquoise freshwater lagoons. The sand is so pure it blocks infiltration, and rainwater fills the hollows between dunes—hence the name "sheets" (lençóis) referring to hung laundry. From June to September, the lagoons are full: that's THE moment. Lagoa Azul and Lagoa Bonita are the two most accessible from Barreirinhas.

The Rio Preguiças crosses Barreirinhas and flows down to the ocean. If your day is too short for the dunes, there's a lancha excursion on the river to Caburé and Mandacaru, two fishing villages between dunes and mangrove. At the Mandacaru lighthouse (32 meters), you climb 198 steps for a 360° view of the park—one of the trip's most striking viewpoints. Combine or alternate with the dunes depending on available time.

activities.text: Book a 4x4 with driver to reach Lagoa Azul then Lagoa Bonita late afternoon. The 4x4 drops you at the park edge, then you walk 15 to 20 minutes through the dunes to reach the first lagoon. Water at 28°C, white sand bottom, average depth of 1.5 meters—you can lie on your back without touching. Stay for sunset on the ridges: pink, orange, violet in less than 30 minutes. Unforgettable, and I mean it.

If you're leaving tomorrow, dine at a local restaurant in Barreirinhas. The Maranhense specialty is arroz de cuxá: rice with Amazonian sorrel leaves, dried shrimp, sesame. Strange, dark green, surprising. Restaurant Terral in Barreirinhas makes a good version. You leave with a taste that sums up your Northeast crossing: intense, unexpected, memorable.

seo.h1_title: Fortaleza, Jericoacoara & Gateway to the Lençóis: 7 Days on Brazil's Northeast Coast
seo.intro_paragraph: Seven days and three Brazilian states (Ceará, Piauí, Maranhão) between red cliffs, shifting dunes, turquoise lagoons, and an Amazonian delta. From Praia do Futuro to the Lagoa Azul of the Lençóis, via the buggies of Canoa Quebrada, the sunsets of Jericoacoara, and the 4x4 crossing to the Parnaíba Delta.
seo.seo_hook: Canoa Quebrada's cliffs, Jeri's Sunset Dune, 4x4 crossing to Parnaíba, Amazonian delta, and the first lagoons of the Lençóis: Brazil's Northeast coast in 7 days.
featured_list: Fortaleza: Mercado Central, Brazil's largest hammock producer
Canoa Quebrada: ochre-red cliffs and dune buggy tours
Jericoacoara: village without electricity until 1998, ranked top 10 beaches worldwide
Lençóis Maranhenses: 1,550 km² of white dunes and freshwater lagoons
